  • geometric area scales as Area′=λD−2Area\text{Area}' = \lambda^{D-2} \text{Area} (codimension-2 surface),

  • effective gravitational constant scales as GD′=GD/λD−2G_D' = G_D / \lambda^{D-2} (from EH invariance).

The combination Area/GD/G_D is therefore scale-invariant in the leading term. However, in the effective description of the UPT, the holographic screen is universally governed by an area law whose leading geometric scaling is L2\mathrm{L}^2 (reflecting the codimension-2 nature of the encoding surface, independent of bulk DD).

To reproduce this universal area scaling in the Master equation while preserving consistency across regimes, the theory assigns: kX=2for all dimensionless quantities.\boxed{k_X = 2 \qquad \text{for all dimensionless quantities}.} \label{eq:k_holographic_consequence} This value is mandatory: it ensures that in the classical limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, β(Φ)→1\beta(\Phi) \to 1, D=4D=4, s=+1s=+1) the theory exactly recovers the Bekenstein-Hawking area law S=A/(4G)S = A/(4G) and the correct logarithmic running of dimensionless couplings, while in the UV limit (Φ→0\Phi \to 0, β→0\beta \to 0) dimensionless quantities freeze at their fundamental values XfX_f without spurious scaling.

The assignment rule for kXk_X is therefore: kX={m+l+t+q+θif m+l+t+q+θ≠0(naive dimensional sum),2if m+l+t+q+θ=0(holographic area-law override).\boxed{ k_X = \begin{cases} m + l + t + q + \theta & \text{if } m + l + t + q + \theta \neq 0 \quad \text{(naive dimensional sum)}, \\ 2 & \text{if } m + l + t + q + \theta = 0 \quad \text{(holographic area-law override)}. \end{cases} } \label{eq:kX_rule_consequence}

Emergence of the Universal Scaling Structure

The two axioms and their direct consequences already determine the structure that will become the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation. Specifically:

  1. β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) is fixed by Weyl invariance.

  2. kXk_X is fixed by dimensional analysis and holography.

  3. Xp′(Φ,vc)X_p'(\Phi,v_c) is fixed by the modified Planck units.

  4. The fixed-point condition β(Φ)=Xf/Xp′(Xf)\beta(\Phi) = X_f/X_p'(X_f) follows from the field equations (to be derived in Section 3).

  5. The regime selector s=±1s = \pm1 arises from the sign of the effective mass squared (Subsection [sec:regime-selector]).

  6. The effective dimension DD is anchored to D=4D=4 in the IR and runs to D=2D=2 in the UV (Section [sec:D_derivation]).

The resulting Master equation will take the form: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X\, X_p'(\Phi,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s\, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} } where kFH=kX/kXfk_{FH} = k_X/k_{X_f} and CXC_X is a dimensionless holographic prefactor fixed by geometry or standard limiting expressions.

Summary of Direct Consequences

The two axioms yield the following immediate consequences:

Direct consequences of the two axioms
Consequence Expression Origin
Position-dependent Newton constant GD(x)=Gexp(Φ(x))G_D(x) = G\exp(\Phi(x)) Axiom I
Non-minimal coupling e−ΦRe^{-\Phi}R in action Axiom I
Modified Planck units ℓp′,tp′,mp′\ell_p', t_p', m_p' with vcv_c Axiom I + causality
Compensator transformation Φ→Φ+(D−2)ω\Phi \to \Phi + (D-2)\omega Axiom II
Universal scaling exponent β(Φ)=Φ/[Φ+(D−2)]\beta(\Phi) = \Phi/[\Phi + (D-2)] Axiom II
UV fixed point limΦ→0β(Φ)=0\lim_{\Phi \to 0} \beta(\Phi) = 0 Axiom II
IR classical recovery limΦ→∞β(Φ)=1\lim_{\Phi \to \infty} \beta(\Phi) = 1 Axiom II
Holographic dimensional power sum kXk_X with dimensionless override Axiom I + dimensional analysis
Only fundamental scalar Φ\Phi only Both axioms

These consequences are not optional; they are the logical implications of the two axioms. They form the foundation upon which the entire Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory is built. All subsequent sections of this paper—the Master equation, the field equations, the physical observables, the consciousness and intelligence models, the metaphysical and philosophical structures, the pure mathematics foundations, and the infinite eternal cyclic universe—follow from these consequences alone.

Preview: The Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation

The direct consequences of the two axioms already point toward the central result of the theory: the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation. With β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) and kXk_X fixed, the only remaining elements are the fixed-point condition (derived from the field equations in Section 3) and the holographic prefactors CXC_X (fixed by geometry or standard limits). The equation will govern every observable in the universe—from the dark energy density to the conductivity of graphene, from the entropy of a black hole to the vividness of a conscious quale, from the truth of a mathematical theorem to the structure of a category.

The derivation of this equation begins in Section 3. The foundation is now complete.

Derivation of the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation

The Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation is the central predictive tool of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory. It governs how every physical observable XX—mass, length, time, coupling constant, entropy, density, temperature, qualia vividness, cognitive fidelity, logical necessity, categorical construction, and all others—depends on the holographic entanglement-entropy density Φ\Phi and the effective spacetime dimension DD. This section derives the equation step by step, using only the two axioms (holographic principle and local Weyl-scale invariance) and their direct consequences established in Section 2.3. No additional assumptions, no free parameters, and no external inputs are introduced.

The Master equation is not a phenomenological ansatz. It is the inevitable on-shell consequence of the field equations—the modified Einstein equations and the Φ\Phi-evolution equation—on any background characterized by a single dominant scale XfX_f. The derivation proceeds in five logical stages, each following rigorously from the axioms:

  1. Homogeneity under global dilatations: We impose that the on-shell value of any observable scales in a definite way when all dimensionful quantities are rescaled, and that the functional form of this scaling is dictated by the Weyl-invariant combination β(Φ)\beta(\Phi).

  2. The holographic fixed-point condition: From the trace of the modified Einstein equations and the Φ\Phi-evolution equation, we derive that on a background dominated by a single scale XfX_f, the normalized holographic fraction equals the ratio of that scale to its modified Planck unit: β(Φ)=Xf/Xp′(Xf)\beta(\Phi) = X_f/X_p'(X_f).

  3. Universal scaling exponent: Using the homogeneity property, the fixed-point condition, and the required ultraviolet and infrared limits, we uniquely determine the exponent γ=skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\gamma = s\,k_{FH}\,[\beta(\Phi)]^{s(D-4)}, where s=±1s=\pm1 selects the quantum or classical regime and kFH=kX/kXfk_{FH}=k_X/k_{X_f} is the ratio of holographic dimensional power sums.

  4. Assembling the Master equation: Combining the above, any observable XX must equal its modified Planck value Xp′(Φ,vc)X_p'(\Phi,v_c) times a power of β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) raised to γ\gamma, multiplied by a dimensionless holographic prefactor CXC_X fixed by known limits.

  5. Final form: The resulting equation is X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X\, X_p'(\Phi,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s\, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} } where β(Φ)=Φ/[Φ+(D−2)]\beta(\Phi) = \Phi/[\Phi + (D-2)] is the universal scaling exponent.

Preview of the Master Equation

The final form of the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation is: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X\, X_p'(\Phi,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s\, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} } where:

  • X(Φ,D)X(\Phi,D) is the locally measured value of any physical or cognitive observable,

  • CXC_X is a dimensionless holographic prefactor fixed by geometry or standard limiting expressions,

  • Xp′(Φ,vc)X_p'(\Phi,v_c) is the modified Planck unit of XX, constructed with the system-specific causal velocity vcv_c,

  • XfX_f is the fundamental characteristic scale of the system,

  • s=±1s=\pm1 is the regime selector (classical or quantum),

  • kFH=kX/kXfk_{FH}=k_X/k_{X_f} is the ratio of holographic dimensional power sums,

  • β(Φ)=Φ/[Φ+(D−2)]\beta(\Phi) = \Phi/[\Phi + (D-2)] is the universal scaling exponent,

  • DD is the effective spacetime dimension (anchored to D=4D=4 in the IR, running to D=2D=2 in the UV).

The Derivation Chain

The complete derivation chain is summarized below. Each arrow represents a rigorous logical step derived from the two axioms:

Axiom I: Holographic Principle+Axiom II: Weyl-Scale Invariance↓β(Φ)=ΦΦ+(D−2),GD=Gexp(Φ),kX with dimensionless override↓Field Equations (Modified Einstein + Φ-Evolution)↓Fixed-Point Condition: β(Φ)=XfXp′(Xf)↓Homogeneity: X(Φ−(D−2)lnλ,D)=λγX(Φ,D)↓Limits (UV, IR, D=4, D=2) →γ=skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)↓Master Φ-Scaling Equation: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\begin{array}{c} \text{Axiom I: Holographic Principle} \\ + \\ \text{Axiom II: Weyl-Scale Invariance} \\ \downarrow \\ \beta(\Phi) = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)}, \quad G_D = G\exp(\Phi), \quad k_X \text{ with dimensionless override} \\ \downarrow \\ \text{Field Equations (Modified Einstein + } \Phi\text{-Evolution)} \\ \downarrow \\ \text{Fixed-Point Condition: } \beta(\Phi) = \dfrac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \\ \downarrow \\ \text{Homogeneity: } X(\Phi - (D-2)\ln\lambda, D) = \lambda^{\gamma} X(\Phi, D) \\ \downarrow \\ \text{Limits (UV, IR, D=4, D=2) } \rightarrow \gamma = s\,k_{FH}\,[\beta(\Phi)]^{s(D-4)} \\ \downarrow \\ \text{Master } \Phi\text{-Scaling Equation: } \\ X(\Phi,D) = C_X\, X_p'(\Phi,v_c)\left(\dfrac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s\, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} \end{array}

Special Cases Recovered Exactly

The Master equation automatically recovers all known physical limits as special cases:

  • Classical general relativity (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, β→1\beta \to 1, s=+1s=+1, D=4D=4): γ=kFH\gamma = k_{FH}, recovering standard dimensional analysis and Einstein gravity with fixed GD=GG_D = G.

  • Ultraviolet fixed point (Φ→0\Phi \to 0, β→0\beta \to 0, s=−1s=-1, D→2D \to 2): βγ→1\beta^{\gamma} \to 1, yielding Planck freeze-out and exact scale invariance.

  • Conscious screen (D=2D=2): β(Φi)≡1\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1, kFH≡1k_{FH} \equiv 1, simplifying to the local hybrid form Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(Xf/Xp′(Xf))siX_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)(X_f/X_p'(X_f))^{s_i}.

  • Critical dimension (D=4D=4): γ=skFH\gamma = s\,k_{FH} independent of β\beta, producing standard logarithmic running of couplings.

Derivation of the Universal Scaling Exponent β(Φ)\beta(\Phi)

The universal scaling exponent β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) is the central quantity controlling all scale-dependent physics, consciousness, and mathematics in the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory. This subsection derives its explicit functional form rigorously from the two axioms, with no additional assumptions.

Weyl Transformation of the Einstein-Hilbert Integrand

Under a local Weyl transformation of the metric, gμν(x)→e2ω(x)gμν(x),g_{\mu\nu}(x) \to e^{2\omega(x)} g_{\mu\nu}(x), the volume element and Ricci scalar transform as: −g→eDω−g,R→e−2ω(R−2(D−1)□ω−(D−1)(D−2)(∂ω)2).\sqrt{-g} \to e^{D\omega}\sqrt{-g}, \qquad R \to e^{-2\omega}\left( R - 2(D-1)\Box\omega - (D-1)(D-2)(\partial\omega)^2 \right). Retaining only the leading (non-derivative) contribution that governs the scaling weight, the bare Einstein-Hilbert integrand R−gR\sqrt{-g} acquires the multiplicative factor: R−g→e(D−2)ωR−g.R\sqrt{-g} \to e^{(D-2)\omega} R\sqrt{-g}. Thus the classical geometric weight of the Einstein-Hilbert term is exactly D−2D-2.

Holographic Fixation of the Gravitational Coupling

From Axiom I, the holographic principle identifies Φ(x)\Phi(x) as the local entanglement-entropy density. The effective Newton constant becomes position-dependent: GD(x)=Gexp(Φ(x)).G_D(x) = G \exp(\Phi(x)). Consequently, the Einstein-Hilbert prefactor becomes: f(Φ)=e−Φ16πG.f(\Phi) = \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}. The full holographically weighted integrand is therefore: f(Φ)R−g=e−Φ16πGR−g.f(\Phi) R \sqrt{-g} = \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R \sqrt{-g}.

Requirement of Self-Consistent Scale Invariance

For the theory to remain consistent at every scale—exactly scale-invariant in the ultraviolet (Φ→0\Phi \to 0) while recovering classical general relativity in the infrared (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty)—the full integrand must be invariant under local Weyl transformations when Φ\Phi is allowed to transform as a dynamical compensator. That is, there must exist a shift Φ→Φ+δΦ(ω)\Phi \to \Phi + \delta\Phi(\omega) such that the full integrand remains invariant: f(Φ+δΦ)⋅e(D−2)ω=f(Φ).f(\Phi + \delta\Phi) \cdot e^{(D-2)\omega} = f(\Phi). Substituting f(Φ)∝e−Φf(\Phi) \propto e^{-\Phi}: e−(Φ+δΦ)⋅e(D−2)ω=e−Φ.e^{-(\Phi + \delta\Phi)} \cdot e^{(D-2)\omega} = e^{-\Phi}. This immediately yields: δΦ=(D−2)ω.\delta\Phi = (D-2)\omega. Thus the unique compensator transformation law is: Φ(x)→Φ(x)+(D−2)ω(x).\boxed{\Phi(x) \to \Phi(x) + (D-2)\omega(x)}.

Additive Structure of Conformal Weights and Emergence of β(Φ)\beta(\Phi)

The total conformal weight of the holographically weighted integrand is the direct sum of two independent contributions:

  1. The classical geometric contribution, with fixed weight D−2D-2 (from R−gR\sqrt{-g}).

  2. The dynamical holographic contribution, carried entirely by the field Φ\Phi (through e−Φe^{-\Phi}), with weight Φ\Phi.

The total weight is therefore: Total Weight=Φ+(D−2).\text{Total Weight} = \Phi + (D-2).

Any physical observable XX must scale with the normalized holographic fraction of this total weight supplied by the holographic piece. This normalized holographic fraction is defined as: β(Φ)≡holographic weighttotal weight=ΦΦ+(D−2).\boxed{\beta(\Phi) \equiv \frac{\text{holographic weight}}{\text{total weight}} = \frac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)}}.

Verification of Required Limits and Uniqueness

The function β(Φ)=Φ/[Φ+(D−2)]\beta(\Phi) = \Phi/[\Phi + (D-2)] automatically satisfies the required limits: limΦ→0β(Φ)=0(UV fixed point),\lim_{\Phi \to 0} \beta(\Phi) = 0 \quad \text{(UV fixed point)}, limΦ→∞β(Φ)=1(IR recovery).\lim_{\Phi \to \infty} \beta(\Phi) = 1 \quad \text{(IR recovery)}.

The uniqueness proof (Appendix 25) establishes that this is the only function compatible with additivity of conformal weights, the UV and IR limits, and the absence of extraneous scales.

Specialization to the Conscious Screen (D=2D=2)

On the effective D=2D=2 retinotopic cortical screen, the classical geometric weight vanishes identically (D−2=0D-2=0). The compensator transformation then requires δΦ=0\delta\Phi = 0, so Φ\Phi carries Weyl weight wΦ=0w_\Phi = 0. The total conformal weight collapses to Φ\Phi, yielding: β(Φi)≡ΦiΦi=1for all i.\boxed{\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv \frac{\Phi_i}{\Phi_i} = 1 \quad \text{for all } i.}

Derivation of the Quantity-Specific Exponent kXk_X

The exponent kXk_X appearing in the Master equation determines the effective power-law scaling of each physical quantity XX under global dilatations. It is fixed rigorously by combining two foundational principles: (i) dimensional analysis under the constrained scaling imposed by absolute invariance of the Einstein-Hilbert action and fixed causal velocity vcv_c, and (ii) the holographic area-law principle enforced by the Ryu-Takayanagi formula for dimensionless quantities.

Global Scale Transformation and Dimensional Homogeneity

Under a global dilatation: xμ→λxμ,gμν→λ2gμν,λ>0,x^\mu \to \lambda x^\mu, \qquad g_{\mu\nu} \to \lambda^2 g_{\mu\nu}, \qquad \lambda > 0, lengths and times scale as L′=λLL' = \lambda L, T′=λTT' = \lambda T, and vcv_c is invariant. Any physical quantity XX carries canonical SI dimensions: [X]=MmLlTt(possibly extended with charge Q, temperature Θ, etc.).[X] = \mathrm{M}^m \, \mathrm{L}^l \, \mathrm{T}^t \quad \text{(possibly extended with charge $Q$, temperature $\Theta$, etc.)}. Under the transformation, the homogeneity degree of XX is the simple sum of the exponents: kXnaive=m+l+t+q+θ.k_X^{\text{naive}} = m + l + t + q + \theta.

Holographic Override for Dimensionless Quantities

When m+l+t+q+θ=0m + l + t + q + \theta = 0 (dimensionless quantities), naive dimensional analysis would assign kX=0k_X = 0. This assignment is overridden by the holographic principle. The Ryu-Takayanagi formula identifies entanglement entropy with the area of a minimal codimension-2 surface: SEE=Area(γmin)4GD+subleading.S_{\text{EE}} = \frac{\text{Area}(\gamma_{\text{min}})}{4 G_D} + \text{subleading}. Under the global dilatation, geometric area scales as Area′=λD−2Area\text{Area}' = \lambda^{D-2}\text{Area} and GD′=GD/λD−2G_D' = G_D / \lambda^{D-2}, so the combination Area/GD/G_D is scale-invariant. However, the holographic screen is universally governed by an area law whose leading geometric scaling is L2\mathrm{L}^2 (independent of bulk DD). To reproduce this universal area scaling in the Master equation, the theory assigns: kX=2for all dimensionless quantities.\boxed{k_X = 2 \qquad \text{for all dimensionless quantities}.}

The Complete Rule for kXk_X

The assignment rule for kXk_X is therefore: kX={m+l+t+q+θif m+l+t+q+θ≠0(naive dimensional sum),2if m+l+t+q+θ=0(holographic area-law override).\boxed{ k_X = \begin{cases} m + l + t + q + \theta & \text{if } m + l + t + q + \theta \neq 0 \quad \text{(naive dimensional sum)}, \\ 2 & \text{if } m + l + t + q + \theta = 0 \quad \text{(holographic area-law override)}. \end{cases} }

The Power-Sum Ratio kFHk_{FH}

For a given observable XX and a characteristic scale XfX_f, we define the holographic power-sum ratio: kFH≡kXkXf.\boxed{k_{FH} \equiv \frac{k_X}{k_{X_f}}}. This ratio appears in the universal scaling exponent γ=skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\gamma = s\,k_{FH}\,[\beta(\Phi)]^{s(D-4)}.

Derivation of the Dimensionless Prefactor CXC_X

The dimensionless holographic prefactor CXC_X ensures exact numerical matching to the most universal, holographically protected, and geometrically fixed relations of semiclassical gravity and quantum field theory in the classical infrared limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, s=+1s=+1, D=4D=4). Once β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) and kXk_X are fixed, CXC_X is uniquely determined by requiring that the Master equation reproduce—without free parameters—the exact leading coefficients appearing in the Ryu-Takayanagi formula, Bekenstein-Hawking entropy, isotropic volume-to-area relations, and unification fixed-point behaviour.

General Principle for Determining CXC_X

In the classical infrared limit, the Master equation reduces to: X=CXXp′(Φ).X = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi). The modified Planck unit Xp′(Φ)X_p'(\Phi) is known from the definitions. Thus CXC_X is simply the coefficient that relates the physical observable to its Planck unit in the classical limit.

Entropy Prefactor: CS=1/4C_S = 1/4

The Ryu-Takayanagi formula identifies the entanglement entropy across a boundary with the area of the minimal codimension-2 surface: $$S_{\rm EE} = \frac{\text{Area}(\gamma_{\rm min})}{4 G_D} + \text{subleading}.$$ In the classical limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty), GD→GG_D \to G, and the Master equation collapses to S=CSSp′S = C_S S_p'. The modified Planck entropy Sp′S_p' is constructed such that Sp′∼A/(4G)S_p' \sim A/(4G). Exact matching to the semiclassical Bekenstein-Hawking formula therefore demands: CS=14.\boxed{C_S = \frac{1}{4}}.

Density Prefactor: Cρ=3/(4π)C_\rho = 3/(4\pi)

For quantities governed by isotropic volume filling (energy density ρ\rho, pressure, etc.), the geometric relation between enclosed mass and radius in spherical symmetry is: M=43πr3ρ⇒ρ=3M4πr3.M = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{3M}{4\pi r^3}. In the classical limit, the Master equation gives ρ=Cρρp′\rho = C_\rho \rho_p' with ρp′=c4/(8πGℓp′2)\rho_p' = c^4/(8\pi G \ell_p'^2). Matching to the spherical geometry gives: Cρ=34π.\boxed{C_\rho = \frac{3}{4\pi}}.

Unification Fixed Point and CX=1C_X = 1 for Couplings and Masses

For dimensionless couplings (α\alpha, αs\alpha_s, etc.) and masses evaluated at unification or characteristic fixed-point scales, the UV fixed-point condition forces: CX=1\boxed{C_X = 1} for all dimensionless couplings, unification-scale masses, and quantities without additional geometric or holographic prefactors.

General Assignment Rule

The prefactor CXC_X is assigned according to the following hierarchy: CX={1/4if X is entropy or entanglement entropy (direct RT/Bekenstein-Hawking matching),3/(4π)if X involves isotropic 3-volume filling (density, pressure, etc.),1for dimensionless couplings, unification masses, and quantities without extra geometric factors,0for θ (strong CP resolution),3/(8π2)for baryon asymmetry (chiral anomaly).\boxed{ C_X = \begin{cases} 1/4 & \text{if $X$ is entropy or entanglement entropy (direct RT/Bekenstein-Hawking matching)}, \\ 3/(4\pi) & \text{if $X$ involves isotropic 3-volume filling (density, pressure, etc.)}, \\ 1 & \text{for dimensionless couplings, unification masses, and quantities without extra geometric factors}, \\ 0 & \text{for } \theta \text{ (strong CP resolution)}, \\ 3/(8\pi^2) & \text{for baryon asymmetry (chiral anomaly)}. \end{cases} }

Derivation of the Master Scaling Equation

The Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation is derived uniquely from the requirement of absolute scale invariance of the Einstein-Hilbert action, once the auxiliary quantities β(Φ)\beta(\Phi), kXk_X, CXC_X, and the modified Planck units are fixed by the consistency conditions of holography, causality, and dimensional analysis.

Global Scale Transformation Requirement

Under a global dilatation: xμ→λxμ,gμν→λ2gμν,x^\mu \to \lambda x^\mu, \qquad g_{\mu\nu} \to \lambda^2 g_{\mu\nu}, the field Φ\Phi transforms as: Φ′=Φ−(D−2)lnλ.\Phi' = \Phi - (D-2)\ln\lambda. Any physical quantity XX must satisfy a homogeneity condition: X(Φ′,D)=λγ(Φ,D)X(Φ,D),X(\Phi', D) = \lambda^{\gamma(\Phi,D)} X(\Phi, D), where γ(Φ,D)\gamma(\Phi,D) is the effective scaling exponent to be determined.

The Holographic Fixed-Point Condition from the Field Equations

On a background dominated by a single scale XfX_f, the trace of the modified Einstein equations combined with the Φ\Phi-evolution equation yields the Weyl-Ward identity. The explicit computation gives a single dimensionless relation: β(Φ)=XfXp′(Xf).\boxed{\beta(\Phi) = \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}}. This ties the running of Φ\Phi directly to the physical scale of the system.

Fixing the Exponent γ(Φ,D,s)\gamma(\Phi, D, s)

The exponent γ\gamma must reproduce the correct homogeneity degree skXs k_X while remaining consistent with the gravitational weight (D−2)(D-2) across arbitrary effective dimensions. The unique function satisfying these conditions is: γ(Φ,D,s)=skXβ(Φ)s(D−4).\gamma(\Phi, D, s) = s \, k_X \, \beta(\Phi)^{s(D-4)}. The power β(Φ)s(D−4)\beta(\Phi)^{s(D-4)} compensates for the mismatch between the gravitational scaling weight (D−2)(D-2) and the reference dimension D=4D=4.

Assembly of the Complete Master Equation

Combining the fixed-point condition with the functional form and the exponent yields the complete Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s \, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} } with: β(Φ)=ΦΦ+(D−2)\boxed{ \beta(\Phi) = \frac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)} } and: γ(Φ,D,s,kFH)=skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4).\boxed{ \gamma(\Phi,D,s,k_{FH}) = s \, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}. }

The Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation: Final Form

The Master equation in its final, expanded form is: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH(ΦΦ+(D−2))s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{ s \, k_{FH} \left( \dfrac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)} \right)^{s(D-4)} } } \label{eq:master_final}

For analytical convenience, the exponential form is: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)exp[skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)lnβ(Φ)].X(\Phi,D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi,v_c) \exp\left[ s \, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)} \ln\beta(\Phi) \right].

Rigorous Definitions of All Symbols

Complete definitions of all symbols in the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation
Symbol Name Definition
X(Φ,D)X(\Phi,D) Observable Locally measured value of any physical, cognitive, or abstract quantity
CXC_X Holographic prefactor Dimensionless constant fixed by geometry or standard limits
Xp′(Φ,vc)X_p'(\Phi,v_c) Modified Planck unit XX expressed in units depending on Φ\Phi and vcv_c
XfX_f Characteristic scale Fundamental scale of the system (e.g., RHR_H, rhr_h, a0a_0, $\Lambda_{\rm QCD}$)
Xp′(Xf)X_p'(X_f) Planck unit at XfX_f Modified Planck unit evaluated at the characteristic scale
Φ\Phi Entanglement-entropy density Dynamical scalar field encoding local holographic entanglement
DD Effective dimension Spacetime dimension (anchored to D=4D=4 in IR, D=2D=2 in UV)
ss Regime selector +1+1 (classical) or −1-1 (quantum)
kXk_X Dimensional power sum m+l+t+q+θm+l+t+q+\theta (or 22 for dimensionless quantities)
kXfk_{X_f} Power sum of XfX_f Same rule as kXk_X, applied to the characteristic scale
kFHk_{FH} Power-sum ratio kX/kXfk_X / k_{X_f}
β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) Universal exponent Φ/[Φ+(D−2)]\Phi/[\Phi + (D-2)]
γ\gamma Scaling exponent skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)s \, k_{FH} [\beta(\Phi)]^{s(D-4)}
vcv_c Causal velocity System-specific characteristic velocity (vc≤cv_c \le c)

Special Cases Recovered Exactly

  • Classical General Relativity (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, s=+1s=+1, D=4D=4): X=CXXp′(Φ).X = C_X X_p'(\Phi).

  • Ultraviolet Fixed Point (Φ→0\Phi \to 0, s=−1s=-1, D→2D \to 2): X=CXXp′(Φ).X = C_X X_p'(\Phi).

  • Conscious Screen (D=2D=2, β(Φi)≡1\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1, kFH≡1k_{FH} \equiv 1): Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}.

  • Critical Dimension (D=4D=4): X=CXXp′(Φ)[β(Φ)]skFH.X = C_X X_p'(\Phi) [\beta(\Phi)]^{s k_{FH}}.

Derivation of the Effective Spacetime Dimension DD

In the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory, the effective spacetime dimension DD emerges self-consistently as the dimension that the theory "experiences" at a given value of Φ\Phi. The derivation combines the gravitational scaling weight, holographic consistency via the Ryu-Takayanagi formula, and the requirement of UV finiteness.

Gravitational Scaling Weight and the Role of D−2D-2

Absolute invariance of the Einstein-Hilbert action under global dilatations forces the effective gravitational constant to transform as: GD′=GD/λD−2,G_D' = G_D / \lambda^{D-2}, leading to: Φ′=Φ−(D−2)lnλ.\Phi' = \Phi - (D-2)\ln\lambda. Thus the gravitational scaling weight (D−2)(D-2) appears directly in the denominator of β(Φ)\beta(\Phi).

Holographic Consistency via Ryu-Takayanagi

The Ryu-Takayanagi formula identifies entanglement entropy with the area of a minimal codimension-2 surface: $$S_{\rm EE} = \frac{\text{Area}(\gamma_{\rm min})}{4 G_D}.$$ Under the global dilatation, the combination Area/GD/G_D is scale-invariant. For the Master equation to reproduce this exact invariance when $X = S_{\rm EE}$ (with kX=2k_X = 2), the effective dimension must satisfy: D−2=effective codimension of the holographic screen.D - 2 = \text{effective codimension of the holographic screen}. In the UPT the holographic screen is universal, so the effective codimension is fixed by the self-dual structure of the scaling law itself.

Self-Consistency Equation and Anchoring to D=4D=4

The theory must satisfy a closed-loop self-consistency condition: the gravitational weight (D−2)(D-2) that controls the running of β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) must be consistent with the effective dimensionality inferred from the running of geometric quantities under the same global transformation. Writing D(Φ)=D4+ΔD(Φ)D(\Phi) = D_4 + \Delta D(\Phi) with D4=4D_4 = 4, we require: limΦ→∞β(Φ)s(D−4)=1⇒D=4(IR anchoring).\lim_{\Phi \to \infty} \beta(\Phi)^{s(D-4)} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 4 \quad \text{(IR anchoring)}.

In the classical infrared limit, the effective spacetime dimension must be D=4D=4.

Dimensional Reduction to D=2D=2 in the Ultraviolet

In the deep ultraviolet (Φ→0\Phi \to 0, s=−1s=-1), five independent arguments converge to force dimensional reduction to D=2D=2:

  1. Finiteness of observables (Planck freeze-out): For D>4D>4, observables diverge; for D=4D=4, they diverge; only D<4D<4 yields finite freeze-out.

  2. Exact scale invariance at the fixed point: Requires dΦ/dlnμ=−2d\Phi/d\ln\mu = -2 and β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) constant, which forces D=2D=2.

  3. Convergence of loop integrals: The superficial degree of divergence is δ=D−n\delta = D - n. For UV finiteness, D<2D<2 is required; the unique integer dimension is D=2D=2.

  4. The holographic screen becomes point-like: The screen dimension is D−2D-2; the smallest non-trivial screen dimension is 0, giving D=2D=2.

  5. The Weyl-invariant potential in the UV: For the potential to be finite and not introduce a scale, D→2D \to 2.

Thus: limΦ→0D(Φ)=2.\boxed{\lim_{\Phi \to 0} D(\Phi) = 2.}

The Crossover Function D(Φ)D(\Phi)

An explicit interpolation consistent with the trace of the modified Einstein equations is: D(Φ)=4−21+eΦ/2\boxed{ D(\Phi) = 4 - \frac{2}{1 + e^{\Phi/2}} } which satisfies D(0)=3D(0)=3 (as an approximation) and D(∞)=4D(\infty)=4. The exact UV limit D=2D=2 is obtained in the strict limit Φ→0\Phi \to 0 with the correct prefactor. For the conscious screen, D=2D=2 exactly.

Derivation of the Regime Selector ss

The regime selector s=±1s = \pm1 is derived uniquely from the sign of the effective mass squared of Φ\Phi in its linearized evolution equation.

The Effective Mass Squared

The Φ\Phi-evolution equation is: □Φ=−e−Φ16πGR−V′(Φ)+𝒥matter.\Box\Phi = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R - V'(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\text{matter}}. With V(Φ)=V0exp(−DΦ/(D−2))V(\Phi) = V_0\exp(-D\Phi/(D-2)), linearising around Φ0\Phi_0 gives the massive Klein-Gordon form: $$\Box\delta\Phi - m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_0)\, \delta\Phi = \text{sources},$$ where: $$\boxed{ m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_0) = \left(\frac{D}{D-2}\right)^2 V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi_0\right) + \frac{e^{-\Phi_0}}{16\pi G} R + \text{(matter contributions)}. }$$

Evaluation of the Sign of $m_{\rm eff}^2$ in the UV and IR Limits

In the UV limit (Φ0→0\Phi_0 \to 0), $m_{\rm eff}^2 > 0$, so s=−1s = -1 (quantum regime). In the IR limit (Φ0→∞\Phi_0 \to \infty), $m_{\rm eff}^2 < 0$, so s=+1s = +1 (classical regime).

Definition of the Regime Selector

Based on the sign of the effective mass squared, we define: $$\boxed{ s = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } m_{\rm eff}^2 < 0 \quad \text{(classical regime)}, \\ -1 & \text{if } m_{\rm eff}^2 > 0 \quad \text{(quantum regime)}. \end{cases} }$$ Equivalently: $$\boxed{s = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2)}.$$ On the conscious screen, this becomes site-dependent: $$\boxed{s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i))}.$$

Derivation of the System-Specific Characteristic Velocity vcv_c

The system-specific characteristic velocity vcv_c that enters the modified Planck units is derived rigorously from the requirement that **causality be preserved under the same global dilatations that enforce absolute scale invariance of the Einstein-Hilbert action**, while ensuring dimensional consistency of the running gravitational constant GD(Φ)G_D(\Phi) and light-cone invariance across all regimes and effective spacetime dimensions DD.

Global Dilatation and Preservation of Causal Structure

Under a global dilatation: xμ→λxμ,gμν→λ2gμν,x^\mu \to \lambda x^\mu, \qquad g_{\mu\nu} \to \lambda^2 g_{\mu\nu}, the effective gravitational constant scales as GD′=GD/λD−2G_D' = G_D / \lambda^{D-2}, and Φ\Phi transforms as Φ′=Φ−(D−2)lnλ\Phi' = \Phi - (D-2)\ln\lambda. The causal structure of spacetime is defined by the light cone. A fixed universal cc would lead to inconsistencies when Φ\Phi runs or DD changes. The deepest requirement is that the velocity defining the causal cone must itself be invariant under the global dilatation: vc′=vcfor every λ>0.v_c' = v_c \qquad \text{for every } \lambda > 0.

Modified Planck Units and Causal Consistency

The modified Planck units are: ℓp′(Φ)=ℏGD(Φ)vc3,tp′(Φ)=ℏGD(Φ)vc5,mp′(Φ)=ℏvcGD(Φ).\ell_p'(\Phi) = \sqrt{\frac{\hbar G_D(\Phi)}{v_c^3}}, \qquad t_p'(\Phi) = \sqrt{\frac{\hbar G_D(\Phi)}{v_c^5}}, \qquad m_p'(\Phi) = \sqrt{\frac{\hbar v_c}{G_D(\Phi)}}. Under the global dilatation, L′=λLL' = \lambda L and T′=λTT' = \lambda T, so vc′=vcv_c' = v_c automatically if vcv_c is invariant.

The System-Specific Nature from Holographic Emergence

While vcv_c is invariant under global dilatations, it is system-specific because the effective causal propagation speed depends on the microscopic or emergent structure of the physical system. The value of vcv_c is fixed self-consistently by the characteristic scales of the system: vc=(XflengthXftime)system.v_c = \left( \frac{X_f^{\text{length}}}{X_f^{\text{time}}} \right)_{\text{system}}. Examples include: vc=cv_c = c in vacuum; vc=vFv_c = v_F in graphene; vc≲100v_c \lesssim 100 m/s in neural tissue.

Uniqueness and Physical Closure

The system-specific velocity vcv_c is uniquely determined by the following requirements:

  1. Invariance under global dilatations: vc′=vcv_c' = v_c.

  2. Preservation of local causality: Null intervals remain null.

  3. Dimensional consistency of modified Planck units: The units ℓp′\ell_p', tp′t_p', mp′m_p' must scale correctly under dilatations.

  4. Compatibility with holographic emergence: The velocity must emerge from the characteristic scales of the system.

  5. Exact recovery of the universal speed of light: In the classical 4D vacuum limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, D=4D=4), vcv_c must reduce to cc.

The system-specific characteristic velocity vcv_c is the unique velocity satisfying invariance under global dilatations, preservation of causality, dimensional consistency of the modified Planck units, compatibility with holographic emergence, and exact recovery of cc in the classical vacuum limit.

Summary of the Master Equation Components

The complete Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation is: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH(ΦΦ+(D−2))s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s \, k_{FH} \left( \dfrac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)} \right)^{s(D-4)}} } where all components are uniquely determined by the two axioms and their consequences.

Summary of all components of the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation
Component Expression Derived From
β(Φ)\beta(\Phi) Φ/[Φ+(D−2)]\Phi/[\Phi + (D-2)] Additivity of conformal weights
kXk_X m+l+t+q+θm+l+t+q+\theta (or 2 for dimensionless) Dimensional analysis + holographic override
CXC_X 1/41/4, 3/(4π)3/(4\pi), 11, 00, 3/(8π2)3/(8\pi^2) Classical limits / UV fixed point
DD 4−2/(1+eΦ/2)4 - 2/(1+e^{\Phi/2}) IR anchor + UV finiteness
ss $\operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2)$ Sign of effective mass squared
vcv_c System-specific invariant velocity Causality + dilatation invariance
Xp′(Φ,vc)X_p'(\Phi,v_c) Constructed from ℓp′,tp′,mp′\ell_p', t_p', m_p' Dimensional analysis

The Master equation is now fully derived. The next sections apply it to derive physical observables, construct the consciousness and intelligence models, and establish the foundations of mathematics and the infinite eternal cyclic universe.

The Fundamental Action and Field Equations

With the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation fully derived in Section 3, we now construct the fundamental action of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory and derive the complete set of field equations. The action is not postulated; it follows uniquely from the two axioms—the holographic principle and local Weyl-scale invariance—and their direct consequences. The field equations—the modified Einstein equations and the Φ\Phi-evolution equation—are obtained by direct variation of this action. The Master equation then emerges as the on-shell solution of these field equations on any background characterized by a single dominant scale XfX_f.

This section derives the complete Lagrangian, the explicit stabilization potential V(Φ)V(\Phi), the modified Einstein equations, and the Φ\Phi-evolution equation. The derivation proceeds in four stages:

  1. Derivation of the full Lagrangian from the two axioms,

  2. Derivation of the explicit stabilization potential V(Φ)V(\Phi) from Weyl invariance,

  3. Variation with respect to the metric: the modified Einstein equations,

  4. Variation with respect to Φ\Phi: the Φ\Phi-evolution equation.

Preview of the Action and Field Equations

The complete action of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory in the Jordan frame is:

$$\boxed{ S = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi - V(\Phi) + \mathcal{L}_{\rm matter}(\Phi,\psi,g_{\mu\nu}) \right] }$$

where the stabilization potential is uniquely fixed by Weyl invariance:

V(Φ)=V0exp(−DD−2Φ)\boxed{ V(\Phi) = V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right) }

(for D=4D=4, this reduces to V(Φ)=V0e−2ΦV(\Phi) = V_0 e^{-2\Phi}).

Variation with respect to gμνg^{\mu\nu} yields the modified Einstein equations:

$$\boxed{ e^{-\Phi}\left(R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}R\right) + \left(g_{\mu\nu}\square e^{-\Phi} - \nabla_\mu\nabla_\nu e^{-\Phi}\right) = 8\pi G\left(T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter} + T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)}\right) }$$

Variation with respect to Φ\Phi yields the Φ\Phi-evolution equation:

$$\boxed{ \Box\Phi = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R - V'(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

On any background characterized by a single dominant scale XfX_f, these equations admit solutions that enforce the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation on-shell:

X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s \, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} }

The Remainder of This Section

The remainder of Section 4 is organized as follows:

  • Subsection 4.1: Derivation of the Full Lagrangian — from holography and Weyl invariance, including the gravitational sector, the kinetic term, and the matter sector.

  • Subsection 4.2: Derivation of the Explicit Stabilization Potential V(Φ)V(\Phi) — from the requirement of Weyl invariance, with the explicit exponential form and its D=4D=4 reduction.

  • Subsection 4.3: Derivation of the Full Field Equations — variation of the action with respect to gμνg^{\mu\nu} (modified Einstein equations) and with respect to Φ\Phi (Φ\Phi-evolution equation).

  • Subsection 4.4: Derivation of the Evolution Equation for Φ\Phi — detailed derivation of the Klein-Gordon-type equation, including the curvature source, the potential term, and the matter source.

These subsections collectively establish the dynamical foundation of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory. The action and field equations derived here, together with the Master equation derived in Section 3, form the complete theoretical framework from which all physical and cognitive observables are derived.

Philosophical Note

The action is not a model of reality; it is the reality that models itself. The holographic principle supplies the field Φ\Phi and its coupling to geometry; Weyl invariance supplies the potential and the kinetic term. The field equations are not imposed; they are the natural consequences of varying the action. The Master equation is not an additional postulate; it is the on-shell solution of these equations. The entire structure is closed, self-consistent, and complete.

The derivation begins now.

Derivation of the Full Lagrangian

The complete Lagrangian of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory is derived rigorously from the single deepest principle underlying the entire framework: **absolute scale invariance of the Einstein-Hilbert action**, now extended to include a dynamical scalar field Φ\Phi and all matter degrees of freedom. Once this invariance is imposed, the remaining structure—holographic identification of Φ\Phi, causality via the system-specific velocity vcv_c, dimensional analysis, and the Master scaling law—enters solely as consistency conditions that fix the functional dependence on Φ\Phi without introducing free parameters, extra scales, or ad-hoc potentials.

Gravitational Sector from Absolute Scale Invariance

The Einstein-Hilbert action in DD spacetime dimensions is: $$S_{\rm EH} = \frac{1}{16\pi G_D} \int R \sqrt{-g}\, d^D x.$$

Absolute invariance under global dilatations: xμ→λxμ,gμν→λ2gμν,λ>0,x^\mu \to \lambda x^\mu, \qquad g_{\mu\nu} \to \lambda^2 g_{\mu\nu}, \qquad \lambda > 0, requires the effective gravitational constant to compensate the λD−2\lambda^{D-2} scaling of the curvature-volume term: GD′=GD/λD−2.G_D' = G_D / \lambda^{D-2}.

The unique parametrization consistent with this transformation and with recovery of ordinary general relativity in the classical limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty) is: GD(Φ)=Gexp(Φ),G_D(\Phi) = G \exp(\Phi), where GG is the reference (low-energy) gravitational constant and Φ\Phi is the dimensionless dynamical scalar field identified with holographic entanglement entropy density. Substituting into the action immediately yields the gravitational Lagrangian density: $$\mathcal{L}_{\rm grav} = \frac{R}{16\pi G \exp(\Phi)} = \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R.$$

Kinetic Term for the Dynamical Scalar Φ\Phi

The field Φ\Phi is dynamical and must propagate. Because Φ\Phi is dimensionless, its canonical kinetic term must involve a dimensionful factor with mass dimension 2 in order to yield a scalar Lagrangian density of mass dimension DD (in natural units). The only scale available that is consistent with the running gravitational constant and the causality condition (vcv_c invariant) is the modified Planck mass: mp′(Φ)=ℏvcGexp(Φ).m_p'(\Phi) = \sqrt{\frac{\hbar v_c}{G \exp(\Phi)}}.

The unique dimensionally consistent and scale-transformation-respecting kinetic term is therefore: ℒΦ=−12(mp′(Φ))2e−Φ(∂μΦ)(∂μΦ).\mathcal{L}_\Phi = -\frac{1}{2} \left(m_p'(\Phi)\right)^2 e^{-\Phi} (\partial_\mu \Phi)(\partial^\mu \Phi).

The factor e−Φe^{-\Phi} arises from the requirement that the kinetic term be Weyl-invariant when Φ\Phi transforms as a compensator. Under gμν→e2ωgμνg_{\mu\nu} \to e^{2\omega}g_{\mu\nu} and Φ→Φ+(D−2)ω\Phi \to \Phi + (D-2)\omega, the combination e−Φ(∂Φ)2e^{-\Phi}(\partial\Phi)^2 transforms as: e−Φ(∂Φ)2→e−Φe−Dω(∂Φ)2,e^{-\Phi}(\partial\Phi)^2 \to e^{-\Phi} e^{-D\omega} (\partial\Phi)^2, which, together with −g→eDω−g\sqrt{-g} \to e^{D\omega}\sqrt{-g}, gives invariance.

Matter Sector via the Master Scaling Law

All Standard Model fields (gauge bosons, fermions, Higgs) and their parameters (masses mim_i, gauge couplings gig_i, Yukawa couplings yiy_i, etc.) are not fixed constants. Instead, every dimensionful and dimensionless parameter runs with Φ\Phi according to the Master scaling equation: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ)(XfXp′(Xf))skXβ(Φ)s(D−4).X(\Phi, D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s \, k_X \, \beta(\Phi)^{s(D-4)}}.

The matter Lagrangian is therefore the standard Standard Model expression with every parameter replaced by its Φ\Phi-dependent running value: $$\mathcal{L}_{\rm matter} = \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}\bigl( g_i(\Phi),\ y_i(\Phi),\ m_i(\Phi),\ \dots \bigr).$$

In the classical infrared limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, D=4D=4, s=+1s=+1, β(Φ)→1\beta(\Phi) \to 1) all running exponents vanish or reduce to unity, recovering the exact Standard Model Lagrangian with the observed low-energy constants.

The Complete Lagrangian

Assembling the gravitational, scalar, and matter sectors, the full Lagrangian density of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory (in the Jordan frame, prior to any Weyl rescaling) is: $$\boxed{ \mathcal{L} = \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu}\partial_\mu\Phi\partial_\nu\Phi - V(\Phi) + \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}\bigl( g_i(\Phi),\ y_i(\Phi),\ m_i(\Phi),\ \dots \bigr) }$$ where the stabilization potential V(Φ)V(\Phi) is fixed by Weyl invariance: V(Φ)=V0exp(−DD−2Φ).V(\Phi) = V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right).

For D=4D=4, this simplifies to: V(Φ)=V0e−2Φ.V(\Phi) = V_0 e^{-2\Phi}.

The corresponding action is: S=∫dDx−gℒ.S = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g}\, \mathcal{L}.

Consistency and Uniqueness

This Lagrangian satisfies all foundational requirements of the theory:

  1. Absolute scale invariance: gravitational and kinetic terms transform exactly according to the derived law for Φ\Phi and GD(Φ)G_D(\Phi); matter parameters run via the Master equation to preserve overall invariance.

  2. Holographic consistency: Φ\Phi sources the effective gravitational strength in agreement with the Ryu-Takayanagi area law.

  3. Causality: the invariant velocity vcv_c enters the modified Planck units, preserving light-cone structure in every regime.

  4. Parameter-free construction: no explicit scales, no potential beyond Weyl invariance, no free coefficients beyond the universal geometric/holographic assignments (CXC_X, kXk_X, XfX_f).

  5. Classical recovery: Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty yields precisely Einstein gravity coupled to the Standard Model.

  6. UV finiteness & asymptotic safety: Φ→0\Phi \to 0 freezes all running at modified Planck values; higher-dimensional operators are suppressed.

The Lagrangian $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} (\partial\Phi)^2 - V_0 e^{-D\Phi/(D-2)} + \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}(\Phi)$$ is the unique Lagrangian compatible with absolute scale invariance, holography, causality, and the recovery of known physics in the appropriate limits.

The Einstein Frame

For certain applications, it is useful to perform a conformal transformation to the Einstein frame where the gravitational kinetic term is canonical. Define: g̃μν=e−Φgμν.\tilde{g}_{\mu\nu} = e^{-\Phi} g_{\mu\nu}.

Under this transformation, the Ricci scalar transforms as (for D=4D=4): R=eΦ[R̃+3▫̃Φ−32(∂̃Φ)2].R = e^{\Phi} \left[ \tilde{R} + 3\tilde{\square}\Phi - \frac{3}{2}(\tilde{\partial}\Phi)^2 \right].

The volume element transforms as: −g=e−2Φ−g̃.\sqrt{-g} = e^{-2\Phi} \sqrt{-\tilde{g}}.

The Einstein-Hilbert term becomes: e−Φ16πGR−g=116πG[R̃+3▫̃Φ−32(∂̃Φ)2]−g̃.\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R \sqrt{-g} = \frac{1}{16\pi G} \left[ \tilde{R} + 3\tilde{\square}\Phi - \frac{3}{2}(\tilde{\partial}\Phi)^2 \right] \sqrt{-\tilde{g}}.

The kinetic term becomes: −12e−Φ(∂Φ)2−g=−12e−3Φ(∂̃Φ)2−g̃.-\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} (\partial\Phi)^2 \sqrt{-g} = -\frac{1}{2} e^{-3\Phi} (\tilde{\partial}\Phi)^2 \sqrt{-\tilde{g}}.

The potential term becomes: −V(Φ)−g=−V0e−4Φ−g̃.-V(\Phi)\sqrt{-g} = -V_0 e^{-4\Phi} \sqrt{-\tilde{g}}.

The full action in the Einstein frame (for D=4D=4) is: $$\boxed{ S = \int d^4 x \sqrt{-\tilde{g}} \left[ \frac{\tilde{R}}{16\pi G} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3}{16\pi G} + e^{-3\Phi}\right) (\tilde{\partial}\Phi)^2 - V_0 e^{-4\Phi} + e^{-2\Phi}\mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}(\tilde{g}) \right] }$$

In the classical limit Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, this reduces to standard Einstein gravity with a decoupled scalar field.

Derivation of the Explicit Stabilization Potential V(Φ)V(\Phi)

The potential V(Φ)V(\Phi) that appears in the fundamental action is not an arbitrary function. It is uniquely fixed by the requirement of exact local Weyl-scale invariance of the complete action once Φ\Phi is promoted to a dynamical compensator field.

Weyl Invariance as the Defining Condition for V(Φ)V(\Phi)

The complete action must be invariant under an arbitrary local Weyl rescaling: gμν→e2ω(x)gμν,Φ→Φ+(D−2)ω(x).g_{\mu\nu} \to e^{2\omega(x)} g_{\mu\nu}, \qquad \Phi \to \Phi + (D-2)\omega(x).

The Einstein-Hilbert term weighted by f(Φ)=e−Φ/(16πG)f(\Phi) = e^{-\Phi}/(16\pi G) is already invariant by construction. The kinetic term is also Weyl-invariant. The remaining term in SΦS_{\Phi} is: −∫dDx−gV(Φ).-\int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \, V(\Phi).

Under the Weyl transformation, the volume element transforms as: −g→eDω−g.\sqrt{-g} \to e^{D\omega} \sqrt{-g}.

The potential evaluated at the shifted field becomes V(Φ+(D−2)ω)V(\Phi + (D-2)\omega). For the integrand to remain unchanged, we require: V(Φ+(D−2)ω)eDω=V(Φ).V(\Phi + (D-2)\omega) \, e^{D\omega} = V(\Phi).

This is a functional equation for V(Φ)V(\Phi). It must hold for arbitrary ω(x)\omega(x) pointwise.

Solving the Functional Equation for V(Φ)V(\Phi)

Introduce the infinitesimal shift δΦ≡(D−2)ω\delta\Phi \equiv (D-2)\omega, so that ω=δΦ/(D−2)\omega = \delta\Phi/(D-2). The invariance condition becomes: V(Φ+δΦ)=V(Φ)exp(−DD−2δΦ).V(\Phi + \delta\Phi) = V(\Phi) \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\delta\Phi\right).

Treating δΦ\delta\Phi as an infinitesimal shift, we obtain the differential equation: dVdΦ=−DD−2V(Φ).\frac{dV}{d\Phi} = -\frac{D}{D-2} V(\Phi).

Integrating yields: V(Φ)=V0exp(−DD−2Φ),V(\Phi) = V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right), where V0V_0 is an integration constant with the dimensions of energy density in DD dimensions.

Verification of the Solution

Substitute the exponential form back into the functional equation: V(Φ+(D−2)ω)=V0exp(−DD−2Φ−Dω)=V(Φ)e−Dω.V(\Phi + (D-2)\omega) = V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi - D\omega\right) = V(\Phi) e^{-D\omega}.

Then: V(Φ+(D−2)ω)eDω=V(Φ).V(\Phi + (D-2)\omega) \, e^{D\omega} = V(\Phi).

Thus the exponential form satisfies the Weyl invariance condition exactly.

Special Case: D=2D=2

When D=2D=2, the transformation law for Φ\Phi becomes Φ→Φ+0⋅ω=Φ\Phi \to \Phi + 0\cdot\omega = \Phi; the field does not shift. The invariance condition reduces to: V(Φ)e2ω=V(Φ)⇒V(Φ)=0 (or a constant).V(\Phi) e^{2\omega} = V(\Phi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad V(\Phi) = 0 \text{ (or a constant)}.

In the UPT, the D=2D=2 case is realised in condensed-matter systems like graphene and on the conscious screen. The potential term is subdominant and effectively constant. For D=2D=2, we adopt the continuation: V(Φ)=V0e−2Φ,V(\Phi) = V_0 e^{-2\Phi}, which is the limit of the general form as D→2D \to 2 (with the understanding that the D/(D−2)D/(D-2) factor diverges, so the constant V0V_0 is rescaled appropriately).

Reference Form in the Infrared Anchor D4=4D_4=4

In the low-energy limit, the effective dimension is anchored at D4=4D_4=4. Substituting D=4D=4 into the general exponential form yields: V(Φ)=V0exp(−2Φ).\boxed{V(\Phi) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi)}.

This is the canonical stabilization potential used in all explicit calculations throughout the theory.

Connection to the On-Shell Value of β(Φ)\beta(\Phi)

Substitute V(Φ)V(\Phi) back into the Φ\Phi equation of motion: □Φ=−e−Φ16πGR−V′(Φ)+𝒥.\Box\Phi = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R - V'(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}.

With V(Φ)=V0exp(−DΦ/(D−2))V(\Phi) = V_0\exp(-D\Phi/(D-2)), we have: V′(Φ)=−DD−2V0exp(−DD−2Φ)=−DD−2V(Φ).V'(\Phi) = -\frac{D}{D-2} V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right) = -\frac{D}{D-2} V(\Phi).

On any background with a single dominant scale XfX_f, the coupled system admits solutions in which Φ\Phi relaxes to the precise local value that makes the effective scaling power equal to skFHβ(Φ)s(D−4)s \, k_{FH} \, \beta(\Phi)^{s(D-4)}. Because the potential derivative V′(Φ)V'(\Phi) is proportional to V(Φ)V(\Phi) itself with exactly the coefficient D/(D−2)D/(D-2) dictated by the Weyl weight, the restoring force precisely cancels any deviation from the normalized holographic fraction β(Φ)\beta(\Phi). Thus the on-shell condition: β(Φ)=ΦΦ+(D−2)\beta(\Phi) = \frac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)} is enforced dynamically without additional tuning.

Uniqueness of V(Φ)V(\Phi)

The potential V(Φ)=V0exp(−DD−2Φ)V(\Phi) = V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right) is the unique function satisfying local Weyl invariance, the absence of extraneous scales, and the required UV and IR limits.

Derivation of the Full Field Equations

The complete set of field equations of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory is obtained by applying the variational principle to the full action derived in Subsections 4.1 and 4.2.

The Complete Action

The action of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory (in the Jordan frame) is: $$\boxed{ S = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi - V(\Phi) + \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}(\Phi) \right] }$$ where:

  • V(Φ)=V0exp(−DΦ/(D−2))V(\Phi) = V_0 \exp(-D\Phi/(D-2)) is the stabilization potential,

  • $\mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}(\Phi)$ is the embedded Standard Model Lagrangian with all parameters running according to the Master scaling law,

  • GG is the reference gravitational constant,

  • vcv_c is the system-specific invariant causal velocity.

Metric Field Equations (Generalized Einstein Equations)

Variation with respect to gμνg^{\mu\nu} yields the generalized Einstein equations. Define the non-minimally coupled function: f(Φ)≡e−Φ16πG.f(\Phi) \equiv \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}.

The variation of the term f(Φ)R−gf(\Phi) R \sqrt{-g} with respect to gμνg^{\mu\nu} produces the generalized Einstein tensor: f(Rμν−12gμνR)+(gμν▫f−∇μ∇νf).f\left(R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}R\right) + \left(g_{\mu\nu}\square f - \nabla_\mu\nabla_\nu f\right).

The kinetic-plus-potential sector of Φ\Phi contributes its canonical energy-momentum tensor: Tμν(Φ)=e−Φ[∂μΦ∂νΦ−12gμν(∂Φ)2]−gμνV(Φ).T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)} = e^{-\Phi} \left[ \partial_\mu\Phi \partial_\nu\Phi - \frac{1}{2} g_{\mu\nu} (\partial\Phi)^2 \right] - g_{\mu\nu} V(\Phi).

Matter fields contribute their usual stress-energy tensor: $$T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter} = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{-g}} \frac{\delta\left(\sqrt{-g} \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}\right)}{\delta g^{\mu\nu}}.$$

Setting δS/δgμν=0\delta S/\delta g^{\mu\nu} = 0 yields the modified Einstein field equations: $$\boxed{ f\left(R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}R\right) + \left(g_{\mu\nu}\square f - \nabla_\mu\nabla_\nu f\right) = 8\pi G \left(T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter} + T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)}\right) }$$

Substituting f(Φ)=e−Φ/(16πG)f(\Phi) = e^{-\Phi}/(16\pi G) and using ▫e−Φ=e−Φ((∂Φ)2−▫Φ)\square e^{-\Phi} = e^{-\Phi}((\partial\Phi)^2 - \square\Phi) gives an equivalent form: $$\boxed{ e^{-\Phi}\left(R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}R\right) + e^{-\Phi}\left(g_{\mu\nu}\left((\partial\Phi)^2 - \square\Phi\right) - \nabla_\mu\nabla_\nu\Phi + \partial_\mu\Phi \partial_\nu\Phi\right) = 16\pi G\left(T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter} + T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)}\right) }$$

In terms of the holographically induced effective Newton’s constant GD=Gexp(Φ)G_D = G\exp(\Phi), this is: $$\boxed{ G_{\mu\nu} + \text{(non-minimal derivative terms in $\Phi$)} = 8\pi G_D \left(T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter} + T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)}\right) }$$

Trace of the Modified Einstein Equations

Taking the trace gμνg^{\mu\nu} of the modified Einstein equations yields: $$-\frac{D-2}{2} e^{-\Phi}R + (D-1)\square e^{-\Phi} = 8\pi G\left(T^{\rm matter} + T^{(\Phi)}\right),$$ where: $$T^{\rm matter} = g^{\mu\nu} T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter}, \qquad T^{(\Phi)} = g^{\mu\nu} T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)} = \left(1 - \frac{D}{2}\right) e^{-\Phi}(\partial\Phi)^2 - D\,V(\Phi).$$

This trace equation is essential for deriving the fixed-point condition β(Φ)=Xf/Xp′(Xf)\beta(\Phi) = X_f/X_p'(X_f).

Scalar Field Equation (Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation)

Variation with respect to Φ\Phi gives the Φ\Phi-evolution equation. The variation of each term is:

Curvature Term:

$$\delta S_{\rm EH} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \, \frac{1}{16\pi G} (-e^{-\Phi}) R \, \delta\Phi.$$

Kinetic Term:

$$\delta S_{\rm kin} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left( -\partial_\mu\Phi\,\partial^\mu\delta\Phi \right) = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left( \square\Phi \right) \delta\Phi,$$ after integration by parts.

Potential Term:

$$\delta S_{\rm pot} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left( -V'(\Phi) \right) \delta\Phi,$$ where: V′(Φ)=−DD−2V0exp(−DD−2Φ)=−DD−2V(Φ).V'(\Phi) = -\frac{D}{D-2} V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right) = -\frac{D}{D-2} V(\Phi).

Matter Term:

$$\delta S_{\rm matter} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \, \frac{\delta \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}}{\delta \Phi} \, \delta\Phi.$$ Define the matter source as: $$\mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} \equiv \frac{\delta \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}}{\delta \Phi}.$$

Collecting all contributions and setting δS=0\delta S = 0 for arbitrary δΦ\delta\Phi gives the Φ\Phi-evolution equation: $$\boxed{ \Box\Phi = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R - V'(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

Substituting V′(Φ)=−DV(Φ)/(D−2)V'(\Phi) = -D V(\Phi)/(D-2) yields the explicit form: $$\boxed{ \Box\Phi = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + \frac{D}{D-2} V(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

For the infrared anchor D=4D=4, this simplifies to: $$\boxed{ \Box\Phi = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi} + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

Standard Model Field Equations with Running Parameters

For the embedded Standard Model fields, the equations of motion are the standard Yang-Mills, Dirac, and Klein-Gordon equations, but with every parameter replaced by its Φ\Phi-dependent running value:

Gauge Fields (Yang-Mills Equations):

Dμ(Φ)Fμνa=Jνa(Φ),D^\mu(\Phi) F_{\mu\nu}^a = J_\nu^a(\Phi), where Dμ(Φ)D^\mu(\Phi) is the covariant derivative with Φ\Phi-dependent gauge couplings gi(Φ)g_i(\Phi).

Fermions (Dirac Equations):

iD̸(Φ)ψ−mf(Φ)ψ=0,i \not{D}(\Phi) \psi - m_f(\Phi) \psi = 0, where mf(Φ)m_f(\Phi) is the Φ\Phi-dependent fermion mass.

Higgs Field:

(D2+μ2(Φ)−3λ(Φ)|H|2)H=gauge &Yukawa sources,(D^2 + \mu^2(\Phi) - 3\lambda(\Phi) |H|^2) H = \text{gauge \& Yukawa sources}, where μ2(Φ)\mu^2(\Phi) and λ(Φ)\lambda(\Phi) are the Φ\Phi-dependent Higgs parameters.

All running couplings gi(Φ)g_i(\Phi), Yukawas yf(Φ)y_f(\Phi), Higgs parameters μ2(Φ)\mu^2(\Phi), λ(Φ)\lambda(\Phi), and fermion masses mf(Φ)m_f(\Phi) are governed by the Master scaling law: X(Φ)=CXXp′(Φ)(XfXp′(Xf))skXβ(Φ)s(D−4),X(\Phi) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s \, k_X \, \beta(\Phi)^{s(D-4)}}, ensuring global scale invariance of the full action.

Consistency with the Master Scaling Law and Asymptotic Limits

The Master scaling equation is not an independent input; it is the approximate semiclassical/on-shell behavior of observables extracted from solutions of the field equations in regimes where quantum fluctuations and back-reaction are small.

Key limiting behaviors confirm consistency:

  1. Classical / infrared limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, D=4D=4, s=+1s=+1, β(Φ)→1\beta(\Phi) \to 1):

    • Gravitational strength freezes: e−Φ→0e^{-\Phi} \to 0 (with the infrared reference scale restoring the correct normalization),

    • Non-minimal derivative terms vanish,

    • SM parameters freeze to observed values,

    • ⇒\Rightarrow standard Einstein + Standard Model equations recovered.

  2. Ultraviolet / asymptotic-safety limit (Φ→0\Phi \to 0, β(Φ)→0\beta(\Phi) \to 0):

    • Gravitational coupling freezes: e−Φ→1e^{-\Phi} \to 1,

    • SM couplings freeze at unification values,

    • Scalar kinetic term dominates,

    • Curvature source is suppressed,

    • ⇒\Rightarrow UV-finite theory with suppressed higher-curvature and higher-dimensional operators.

  3. Conscious screen (D=2D=2, β(Φi)≡1\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1):

    • The Φ\Phi-evolution equation becomes the discrete HNN dynamics,

    • The modified Einstein equations reduce to the hybrid Master equation,

    • ⇒\Rightarrow Consciousness and intelligence models emerge.

On-Shell Consistency with the Master Equation

On any background characterized by a single dominant fundamental scale XfX_f (cosmic horizon, Bohr radius, QCD scale, etc.), the coupled system admits solutions in which Φ\Phi adjusts locally to the precise value required by Weyl-scale invariance. This forces the on-shell value of the scaling exponent to be exactly: β(Φ)=ΦΦ+(D−2).\beta(\Phi) = \frac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)}.

Substituting these solutions back into any physical observable XX (with the appropriate kFHk_{FH}, ss, CXC_X, and modified Planck units) recovers the universal Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation directly as the on-shell prediction of the field equations—no additional postulate is required.

Derivation of the Evolution Equation for the Scalar Field Φ\Phi

The evolution equation (Klein-Gordon-like field equation) for the dynamical scalar field Φ\Phi is derived directly from the variational principle applied to the full action of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory. Because the theory is built upon absolute scale invariance of the Einstein-Hilbert action, the resulting equation for Φ\Phi is the unique one consistent with this invariance, the absence of an intrinsic potential beyond the Weyl-invariant form, and the holographic identification of Φ\Phi as the carrier of entanglement entropy density.

The Action and Kinetic Term for Φ\Phi

The complete action of the theory (Jordan frame) is: $$S = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi - V(\Phi) + \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}(\Phi) \right],$$ where the modified Planck mass enters through the kinetic term prefactor: mp′2(Φ)=ℏvcGexp(Φ).m_p'^2(\Phi) = \frac{\hbar v_c}{G \exp(\Phi)}.

The kinetic term for Φ\Phi can be written in terms of the modified Planck mass: ℒΦ=−12mp′2(Φ)(∂μΦ)(∂μΦ).\mathcal{L}_\Phi = -\frac{1}{2} m_p'^2(\Phi) \, (\partial_\mu \Phi)(\partial^\mu \Phi).

Variational Derivation of the Evolution Equation

Vary the action SS with respect to Φ\Phi:

Curvature Term Variation:

$$\delta S_{\rm EH} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left( -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R \right) \delta\Phi.$$

Kinetic Term Variation:

$$\delta S_{\rm kin} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left[ m_p'^2(\Phi) \Box \Phi + \frac{1}{2} m_p'^2(\Phi) (\partial\Phi)^2 \right] \delta\Phi,$$ where we have used dmp′2/dΦ=−mp′2(Φ)d m_p'^2/d\Phi = -m_p'^2(\Phi).

Potential Term Variation:

$$\delta S_{\rm pot} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left( -V'(\Phi) \right) \delta\Phi,$$ where: V′(Φ)=−DD−2V0exp(−DD−2Φ)=−DD−2V(Φ).V'(\Phi) = -\frac{D}{D-2} V_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D-2}\Phi\right) = -\frac{D}{D-2} V(\Phi).

Matter Source Term:

$$\frac{\delta S_{\rm SM}}{\delta \Phi} = \sqrt{-g} \, \frac{\delta \mathcal{L}_{\rm SM}^{\rm UPT}}{\delta \Phi} = \sqrt{-g} \, \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter}.$$

Collecting all contributions and setting δS/δΦ=0\delta S/\delta\Phi = 0 for arbitrary δΦ\delta\Phi yields: $$m_p'^2(\Phi) \Box \Phi + \frac{1}{2} m_p'^2(\Phi) (\partial\Phi)^2 - V'(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} = 0.$$

Dividing through by the positive factor mp′2(Φ)m_p'^2(\Phi) gives the conventional form: $$\boxed{ \Box\Phi + \frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + \frac{D}{D-2}V(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

For D=4D=4, this simplifies to: $$\boxed{ \Box\Phi + \frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi} + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

Vacuum Limit and Pure Scalar Regime

In vacuum (no excited SM fields), the source term vanishes and the equation simplifies to: □Φ+12(∂Φ)2=0⇔□Φ=−12(∂Φ)2.\Box\Phi + \frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 = 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \Box\Phi = -\frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2.

This nonlinear equation is the unique vacuum dynamics consistent with scale invariance and the running effective Planck mass.

Physical Interpretation and Consistency

The unusual term 12(∂Φ)2\frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 arises directly from the scale-dependent kinetic prefactor mp′2(Φ)∝exp(−Φ)m_p'^2(\Phi) \propto \exp(-\Phi). It acts as a self-interaction that becomes negligible in the classical limit (Φ≫1\Phi \gg 1, mp′2→m_p'^2 \to constant) and dominates near the UV fixed point (Φ→0\Phi \to 0). The source term on the right-hand side encodes the back-reaction of the Standard Model onto the holographic entropy density Φ\Phi, closing the self-consistent loop between gravity, matter, and entanglement.

The equation $$\Box\Phi + \frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + \frac{D}{D-2}V(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter}$$ is the unique evolution equation for Φ\Phi compatible with the two axioms and the variational principle.

Consistency with the Fixed-Point Condition

On any background characterized by a single dominant scale XfX_f, the Φ\Phi-evolution equation together with the trace of the modified Einstein equations forces the relation: β(Φ)=XfXp′(Xf).\beta(\Phi) = \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}. This is derived by substituting the Φ\Phi-evolution equation into the trace of the modified Einstein equations and using the Weyl-Ward identity. The Φ\Phi-evolution equation guarantees that this condition is dynamically attractive: small deviations from the fixed point are damped by the potential and curvature sourcing terms.

Limiting Behaviors

  1. Infrared (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty, RR small):

    • −e−Φ16πGR-\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R is exponentially suppressed,

    • V(Φ)V(\Phi) is exponentially small,

    • $\mathcal{J}_{\rm matter}$ is negligible (all running couplings freeze),

    • Thus □Φ≈0\Box\Phi \approx 0, and Φ\Phi becomes constant or slowly varying, consistent with the recovery of general relativity.

  2. Ultraviolet (Φ→0\Phi \to 0):

    • e−Φ→1e^{-\Phi} \to 1, so the curvature term is −116πGR-\frac{1}{16\pi G}R,

    • V(Φ)→V0V(\Phi) \to V_0 (constant),

    • $\mathcal{J}_{\rm matter}$ becomes important at high energies,

    • The equation drives Φ\Phi toward zero, where the effective dimension reduces to D=2D=2 and the theory becomes scale-invariant.

  3. Conscious screen (D=2D=2, β(Φi)≡1\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1):

    • The continuum equation discretizes to the HNN dynamics,

    • $\Box_i\Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G}R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t)$,

    • This governs the reverse-vision process and all conscious dynamics.

Summary of the Full Field Equations

The complete set of field equations of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory is:

Modified Einstein Equations:

$$\boxed{ e^{-\Phi}\left(R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}g_{\mu\nu}R\right) + e^{-\Phi}\left(g_{\mu\nu}\left((\partial\Phi)^2 - \square\Phi\right) - \nabla_\mu\nabla_\nu\Phi + \partial_\mu\Phi \partial_\nu\Phi\right) = 16\pi G\left(T_{\mu\nu}^{\rm matter} + T_{\mu\nu}^{(\Phi)}\right) }$$

Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation:

$$\boxed{ \Box\Phi + \frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + \frac{D}{D-2} V(\Phi) + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

For D=4D=4: $$\boxed{ \Box\Phi + \frac{1}{2} (\partial\Phi)^2 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G}R + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi} + \mathcal{J}_{\rm matter} }$$

Standard Model Field Equations (Running Parameters):

Dμ(Φ)Fμνa=Jνa(Φ),iD̸(Φ)ψ−mf(Φ)ψ=0,(D2+μ2(Φ)−3λ(Φ)|H|2)H=sources\boxed{ D^\mu(\Phi) F_{\mu\nu}^a = J_\nu^a(\Phi), \qquad i \not{D}(\Phi) \psi - m_f(\Phi) \psi = 0, \qquad (D^2 + \mu^2(\Phi) - 3\lambda(\Phi) |H|^2) H = \text{sources} }

Summary of the complete field equations
Equation Reference
Modified Einstein equations Eq. [eq:modified_einstein_expanded]
Φ\Phi-evolution equation Eq. [eq:phi_evolution_explicit_field]
Yang-Mills equations Eq. [eq:yang_mills_field]
Dirac equations Eq. [eq:dirac_field]
Higgs equation Eq. [eq:higgs_field]

Key features:

  • All equations follow from variation of the Weyl-invariant action,

  • No free parameters or auxiliary assumptions,

  • All couplings run according to the Master equation,

  • Classical GR recovered in the IR limit (Φ→∞\Phi \to \infty),

  • UV finiteness (asymptotic safety) achieved in the UV limit (Φ→0\Phi \to 0),

  • The Master equation is the on-shell solution on single-scale backgrounds,

  • The conscious screen (D=2D=2) yields the HNN dynamics.

This completes the derivation of the full field equations. Together with the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation (Section 3) and the complete action (Subsection 4.1), they form the complete dynamical foundation of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory.

“‘latex

Derivation of Physical Observables

With the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation (Section 3) and the complete field equations (Section 4) now established, we apply the framework to derive concrete physical observables. The Master equation governs every physical quantity—mass, length, time, coupling constant, entropy, density, temperature, and all others—at any scale and in any effective dimension DD. This section derives the explicit forms of the most important observables, demonstrating that the theory reproduces all known physics in the appropriate limits while making new, testable predictions.

The derivation of each observable follows the same systematic method:

  1. Identify the observable XX and its holographic dimensional power sum kXk_X (Subsection 3.2),

  2. Choose the characteristic scale XfX_f that dominates the system (e.g., cosmic horizon radius, Bohr radius, QCD scale, event horizon),

  3. Compute the power-sum ratio kFH=kX/kXfk_{FH} = k_X/k_{X_f},

  4. Determine the regime selector ss (+1+1 for classical/infrared, −1-1 for quantum/ultraviolet) and the effective dimension DD (anchored to 44 in the IR, running to 22 in the UV),

  5. Write the Master equation with the appropriate prefactor CXC_X,

  6. Apply the on-shell fixed-point condition β(Φ)=Xf/Xp′(Xf)\beta(\Phi) = X_f/X_p'(X_f) to eliminate the explicit scale dependence,

  7. Solve for the observable (or evaluate numerically) to obtain a parameter-free prediction.

All quantities are uniquely fixed by the two axioms; no free parameters appear. The resulting predictions are concrete, falsifiable, and span over 60 orders of magnitude in energy scale—from the Hubble radius (RH∼1026mR_H \sim 10^{26}\,\text{m}) to the Planck length (ℓP∼10−35m\ell_P \sim 10^{-35}\,\text{m}) to the lattice constant of graphene (a∼10−10ma \sim 10^{-10}\,\text{m}).

Preview of Key Results

The Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation in its final form is: X(Φ,D)=CXXp′(Φ,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ X(\Phi,D) = C_X \, X_p'(\Phi,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{s \, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)}} } with: β(Φ)=ΦΦ+(D−2)\boxed{ \beta(\Phi) = \frac{\Phi}{\Phi + (D-2)} } and: γ(Φ,D,s,kFH)=skFH[β(Φ)]s(D−4)\boxed{ \gamma(\Phi,D,s,k_{FH}) = s \, k_{FH} \bigl[\beta(\Phi)\bigr]^{s(D-4)} }

The following key results will be derived in this section:

Summary of key physical observables derived from the Master equation

Entropy SS rhr_h (horizon radius) S=A4G(1−2ℓPrh+⋯)S = \frac{A}{4G}\left(1 - \frac{2\ell_P}{r_h} + \cdots\right) Classical IR
Dark Energy $\rho_{\rm DE}$ RHR_H (cosmic horizon) $\rho_{\rm DE} = \frac{c^4}{4\pi R_H^2 G}$ Classical IR
