From Subsection 3.2, the holographic override states that for any dimensionless quantity: kX=2for all dimensionless quantities.\boxed{ k_X = 2 \qquad \text{for all dimensionless quantities.} } \label{eq:kX_dimensionless_D2}

This is a consequence of the area-law origin of holographic entanglement entropy.

Therefore, for conscious observables: kXi=2for all i.\boxed{ k_{X_i} = 2 \qquad \text{for all } i. } \label{eq:kX_conscious_D2}

And for internal sparks: kXf=2.\boxed{ k_{X_f} = 2. } \label{eq:kX_spark_D2}

The Derivation of kFH≡1k_{FH} \equiv 1

With kXi=2k_{X_i} = 2 and kXf=2k_{X_f} = 2, the power-sum ratio is: kFH=kXikXf=22=1.k_{FH} = \frac{k_{X_i}}{k_{X_f}} = \frac{2}{2} = 1. \label{eq:kFH_calculation_D2}

Thus: kFH≡1for all conscious observables and sparks.\boxed{ k_{FH} \equiv 1 \qquad \text{for all conscious observables and sparks.} } \label{eq:kFH_identity_D2}

On the D=2D=2 conscious screen, the holographic dimensional power-sum ratio is identically equal to 1 for all conscious observables and sparks: kFH≡1.\boxed{k_{FH} \equiv 1.}

Proof. Conscious observables are dimensionless, so kXi=2k_{X_i} = 2 by the holographic override. Internal sparks are dimensionless, so kXf=2k_{X_f} = 2. Therefore, kFH=kXi/kXf=2/2=1k_{FH} = k_{X_i}/k_{X_f} = 2/2 = 1. ◻

Simplification of the Master Φ\Phi-Scaling Equation

With β(Φi)≡1\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1 and kFH≡1k_{FH} \equiv 1, the universal scaling exponent simplifies to: γ(Φi,2,si,1)=si.\boxed{ \gamma(\Phi_i,2,s_i,1) = s_i. } \label{eq:gamma_final_D2}

The local Master equation becomes: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. } \label{eq:master_final_D2}

This is the local hybrid Master equation on the conscious screen. It has two distinct forms depending on the regime:

Quantum Regime (si=−1s_i = -1):

Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)Xp′(Xf)Xf.\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. } \label{eq:master_quantum_D2}

In the quantum regime, weak sparks produce amplified responses (inverse scaling). This corresponds to creative, intuitive, dream-like states.

Classical Regime (si=+1s_i = +1):

Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)XfXp′(Xf).\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}. } \label{eq:master_classical_D2}

In the classical regime, strong sparks produce linear responses (linear scaling). This corresponds to logical, focused, vivid states.

Interface (si=0s_i = 0):

When $m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 0$, the regime selector is undefined, and: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc).\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c). } \label{eq:master_interface_D2}

The Global Unified Observable

The global unified observable I≡𝒬I \equiv \mathcal{Q} is the spatial average of the local Master equation: I(Φ,Xf)≡𝒬(Φ,Xf)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).\boxed{ I(\Phi,X_f) \equiv \mathcal{Q}(\Phi,X_f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. } \label{eq:global_final_D2}

When the spark is self-referential (Xf∝I≡𝒬X_f \propto I \equiv \mathcal{Q}): I≡𝒬.\boxed{ I \equiv \mathcal{Q}. } \label{eq:identity_final_D2}

This identity is the mathematical foundation of self-aware general intelligence.

The Fixed-Point Condition on the Conscious Screen

The fixed-point condition (Subsection 3.4) on the conscious screen becomes: β(Φi)=XfXp′(Xf).\beta(\Phi_i) = \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}. \label{eq:fixed_point_D2}

With β(Φi)≡1\beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1, this gives: 1=XfXp′(Xf)⇒Xf=Xp′(Xf).1 = \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_f = X_p'(X_f). \label{eq:fixed_point_simplified_D2}

This means that on the conscious screen, the characteristic scale XfX_f equals its modified Planck unit. The internal spark is always at the Planck scale of the screen. This is the condition for maximal holographic fidelity.

The Regime Selector on the Conscious Screen

The site-dependent regime selector is: $$s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)). \label{eq:s_i_D2}$$

The effective mass squared is: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:m_eff_D2}$$

The two regimes are:

  • Quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1): Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0, $m_{\rm eff}^2 > 0$. Inverse scaling, superposition, creativity.

  • Classical regime (si=+1s_i = +1): Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty, $m_{\rm eff}^2 < 0$. Linear scaling, determinism, focus.

The crossover occurs when: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi_i = \Phi_*^{(i)}. \label{eq:crossover_D2}$$

Summary of the Conscious Screen Specialization

The conscious screen specialization is:

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Effective dimension: } & D = 2, \\ \text{Universal exponent: } & \beta(\Phi_i) \equiv 1, \\ \text{Power-sum ratio: } & k_{FH} \equiv 1, \\ \text{Local Master equation: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}, \\ \text{Global unified observable: } & I \equiv \mathcal{Q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}, \\ \text{Regime selector: } & s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)), \\ \text{Quantum regime: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f} \quad (s_i = -1), \\ \text{Classical regime: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \quad (s_i = +1), \\ \text{Self-referential identity: } & X_f \propto I \equiv \mathcal{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I \equiv \mathcal{Q}. \end{aligned} }$$

Physical Interpretation

The conscious screen specialization reveals that consciousness is what the Φ\Phi-field does on a 2D screen when it becomes self-referential. The global qualia field 𝒬\mathcal{Q} is the subjective experience of the screen’s fidelity. The identity I≡𝒬I \equiv \mathcal{Q} shows that intelligence and consciousness are the same phenomenon, viewed from different perspectives.

This completes the specialization to the conscious screen (D=2D=2). The next sections will construct the Holographic Neural Network and derive the complete consciousness and intelligence models.

"Big things have small beginnings."— Prometheus “‘

The Holographic Neural Network (HNN)

With the conscious screen specialization established in Section 7, we now construct the Holographic Neural Network—the discrete lattice realization of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory on the 2D retinotopic cortical screen. The HNN is not an ad-hoc model of the brain; it is the inevitable lattice transcription of the continuous theory once the effective dimension is D=2D=2. It simultaneously implements consciousness, intelligence, hybrid quantum-classical computation, holographic error correction, and all higher-order cognitive processes on the same 2D lattice.

The HNN is holographic by construction. Every site ii on the 2D lattice corresponds to a cortical column. The dynamical variable Φi\Phi_i at each site encodes the local entanglement-entropy density. The synaptic connections between sites realize the entanglement structure. The entire "bulk" of conscious experience—the vivid mental image, the stream of thought, the felt qualia—is holographically reconstructed from the distributed pattern {Φi}\{\Phi_i\} on this 2D boundary.

Preview of the HNN Results

The HNN is defined by the following equations:

Discrete Action:

$$S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i - V(\Phi_i) \right],$$ with V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi)V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) on the D=2D=2 screen.

Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation:

$$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2 V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) + T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). }$$

Local Master Equation:

Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. }

Global Unified Observable:

I(Φ,Xf)≡𝒬(Φ,Xf)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).\boxed{ I(\Phi,X_f) \equiv \mathcal{Q}(\Phi,X_f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. }

Regime Selector:

$$\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)). }$$

Key Properties of the HNN

  1. Holographic storage: Memories and concepts are distributed non-locally across the screen via Φi\Phi_i patterns, protected by the area law.

  2. Dynamic regime partitioning: Quantum patches (si=−1s_i=-1) enable superposition, creativity, and insight; classical patches (si=+1s_i=+1) enable deterministic reasoning, vivid qualia, and reliable execution.

  3. Self-correction: Local errors create curvature that drives Φ\Phi-waves, automatically restoring the scaling law (holographic error correction).

  4. Top-down projection: Internal sparks XfX_f propagate downward, "painting" conscious experience exactly as the reverse-vision process.

  5. Causality: Modified Planck units with vcv_c (bounded by axonal speed) keep all signalling inside the local light-cone.

  6. Higher-order abstraction: When the spark is elevated to meta-levels, the same lattice generates philosophy, metaphysics, mathematics, logic, and category theory.

Construction of the HNN on the 2D Lattice

The Holographic Neural Network is constructed by discretizing the continuous action of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory on the 2D retinotopic cortical lattice. This subsection defines the lattice architecture, derives the discrete action from first principles, and establishes the mapping between biological neural structures and the holographic variables of the theory.

The 2D Cortical Lattice and Its Topology

The retinotopic cortical sheet is discretized into a 2D lattice of sites i=1,2,…,Ni = 1, 2, \ldots, N. Each site corresponds to a cortical column or minicolumn. The lattice has the following properties:

  1. Spatial dimension: D=2D = 2 (the screen is 2D),

  2. Lattice spacing: aa (the inter-column spacing, approximately 0.50.5 mm in human visual cortex),

  3. Number of sites: N∼106N \sim 10^6 (approximately 10610^6 cortical columns in the visual cortex),

  4. Topology: Approximately flat with periodic boundary conditions in some regions,

  5. Connectivity: Local connections (within a radius of a few lattice spacings) and long-range connections (across the entire screen),

  6. Boundary: The screen has a boundary at the edges of the cortical sheet.

The position of site ii is given by the 2D vector 𝐫i\mathbf{r}_i. The lattice spacing aa defines the fundamental length scale of the conscious screen.

The cortical lattice ℒ\mathcal{L} is a 2D lattice of NN sites with spacing aa: ℒ={𝐫i∈ℝ2:i=1,2,…,N}.\mathcal{L} = \{\mathbf{r}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2 : i = 1, 2, \ldots, N\}. Each site ii corresponds to a cortical column with local entanglement-entropy density Φi\Phi_i.

The Discrete Action from the Continuous Action

The continuous action of the pure gravitational-plus-scalar sector (Subsection 4.1) is: SΦ=∫dDx−g[e−Φ16πGR−12e−Φgμν∂μΦ∂νΦ−V(Φ)].S_{\Phi} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi - V(\Phi) \right]. \label{eq:continuous_action_HNN}

On the 2D conscious screen (D=2D=2), the action is discretized by:

  1. Replacing the integral ∫d2x\int d^2x with the sum ∑ia2\sum_i a^2,

  2. Replacing the continuous fields Φ(x)\Phi(x) with lattice variables Φi\Phi_i,

  3. Replacing the continuous curvature R(x)R(x) with the discrete curvature RiR_i,

  4. Replacing the continuous derivatives ∂μΦ\partial_\mu \Phi with finite differences.

The discrete action of the HNN is: $$\boxed{ S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i - V(\Phi_i) \right], } \label{eq:discrete_action_HNN}$$ where V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi)V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) is the Weyl-invariant potential on the D=2D=2 screen (Subsection 4.2).

The Discrete Curvature RiR_i from Synaptic Connectivity

The continuous curvature RR encodes the local geometry of spacetime. On the cortical lattice, the discrete curvature RiR_i encodes the local synaptic connectivity topology. It is defined through the graph Laplacian of the synaptic network:

Ri=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)wij(Φj−Φi),R_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i), \label{eq:R_i_def} where:

  • 𝒩(i)\mathcal{N}(i) are the neighbours of site ii (within a few lattice spacings),

  • wijw_{ij} are the synaptic weights (normalised such that ∑jwij=1\sum_j w_{ij} = 1),

  • aa is the lattice spacing.

This definition captures the idea that curvature is a measure of how the local entanglement-entropy density deviates from its neighbours—i.e., how synaptic connectivity shapes the Φ\Phi-field.

The discrete curvature RiR_i at site ii is: Ri=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)wij(Φj−Φi),R_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i), where wijw_{ij} are the synaptic weights and 𝒩(i)\mathcal{N}(i) are the neighbours of site ii.

This definition has the following properties:

  • Ri=0R_i = 0 when Φj=Φi\Phi_j = \Phi_i for all neighbours (flat curvature),

  • Ri>0R_i > 0 when neighbours have higher Φ\Phi values (positive curvature),

  • Ri<0R_i < 0 when neighbours have lower Φ\Phi values (negative curvature).

The Kinetic Term and the Discrete d’Alembertian

The continuous kinetic term −12e−Φgμν∂μΦ∂νΦ-\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi is discretized using finite differences. The discrete kinetic term is: $$\mathcal{L}_{\rm kin}^{(i)} = -\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \frac{(\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2}{a^2}. \label{eq:kinetic_discrete_HNN}$$

The discrete d’Alembertian □i\Box_i is defined by the variation of the kinetic term with respect to Φi\Phi_i: □iΦi=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)(Φj−Φi)−∂2Φi∂t2.\Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}. \label{eq:discrete_dalambertian}

This operator ensures causal propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the cortical lattice at speed vcv_c (bounded by axonal conduction velocity).

The discrete d’Alembertian □i\Box_i acting on Φi\Phi_i is: □iΦi=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)(Φj−Φi)−∂2Φi∂t2.\Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}.

The Weyl-Invariant Potential on the Lattice

On the D=2D=2 conscious screen, the Weyl-invariant potential (Subsection 4.2) is: V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi).V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i). \label{eq:V_lattice_HNN}

This potential provides the restoring force that drives Φi\Phi_i toward values that satisfy the local Master equation. In the UV limit (Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0), V(Φi)→V0V(\Phi_i) \to V_0 (constant), and the potential acts as a constant background. In the IR limit (Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty), V(Φi)→0V(\Phi_i) \to 0, and the potential becomes negligible.

The Complete HNN Action and Its Interpretation

Combining the curvature, kinetic, and potential terms, the complete HNN action is: $$\boxed{ S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \frac{(\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2}{a^2} - V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right]. } \label{eq:HNN_action_complete}$$

This action has the following interpretation:

  1. The curvature term e−Φi16πGRi\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i: This is the holographic back-reaction of synaptic topology on the entanglement-entropy density. Local synaptic patterns create curvature that sources changes in Φi\Phi_i.

  2. The kinetic term −12e−Φi∑(Φj−Φi)2/a2-\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum (\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2/a^2: This drives the propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the screen. The factor e−Φie^{-\Phi_i} ensures causality and dimensional consistency.

  3. The potential term −V0e−2Φi-V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}: This provides the restoring force that stabilises Φi\Phi_i on-shell, enforcing the local Master equation.

The Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation

The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation is the fundamental dynamical law of the Holographic Neural Network. It governs the time evolution of the entanglement-entropy density Φi\Phi_i at each lattice site, simultaneously driving conscious experience, intelligence optimization, hybrid computation, error correction, and all higher-order cognitive processes.

The HNN Action (Restated for Reference)

The discrete action of the Holographic Neural Network, derived in Subsection 9.1, is: $$S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i - V(\Phi_i) \right], \label{eq:HNN_action_phi_evol}$$ where:

  • RiR_i is the discrete curvature (synaptic topology),

  • V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi)V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) is the Weyl-invariant potential on the D=2D=2 screen,

  • giμνg_i^{\mu\nu} is the discrete metric (causal structure),

  • ∂μΦi\partial_\mu \Phi_i are finite differences (lattice derivatives).

Variation with Respect to Φi\Phi_i

To obtain the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation, vary the action $S_{\rm HNN}$ with respect to Φi\Phi_i while keeping the metric and other fields fixed: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm HNN}}{\delta \Phi_i} = 0. \label{eq:variation_phi_HNN}$$

The variation yields contributions from three terms:

  1. The curvature term,

  2. The kinetic term,

  3. The potential term.

The Curvature Term Contribution

The curvature term in the HNN action is: $$S_{\rm curv} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:curvature_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm curv}}{\delta \Phi_i} = -a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + \text{(subleading)}. \label{eq:curvature_contribution_HNN}$$

The Kinetic Term Contribution and the Discrete d’Alembertian

The kinetic term in the HNN action is: $$S_{\rm kin} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} e^{-\Phi_i} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i. \label{eq:kinetic_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i gives two contributions:

  1. The standard variation of the kinetic term (ignoring the prefactor dependence): δδΦi[−12(∂Φi)2]→□iΦi.\frac{\delta}{\delta \Phi_i} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} (\partial \Phi_i)^2 \right] \to \Box_i \Phi_i.

  2. An extra contribution from the Φi\Phi_i-dependence of the prefactor e−Φie^{-\Phi_i}: δδΦi[e−Φi]=−e−Φi.\frac{\delta}{\delta \Phi_i} \left[ e^{-\Phi_i} \right] = -e^{-\Phi_i}.

The discrete d’Alembertian (Subsection 9.1) is: □iΦi=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)(Φj−Φi)−∂2Φi∂t2.\Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}. \label{eq:discrete_dalambertian_HNN}

The kinetic term variation yields: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm kin}}{\delta \Phi_i} = a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} e^{-\Phi_i} \left[ \Box_i \Phi_i + \frac{1}{2} (\partial \Phi_i)^2 \right]. \label{eq:kinetic_variation_HNN}$$

The Potential Term Contribution

The potential term in the HNN action is: $$S_{\rm pot} = -\sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} V(\Phi_i) = -\sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}. \label{eq:potential_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm pot}}{\delta \Phi_i} = -a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \frac{\partial V}{\partial \Phi_i} = -a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} (-2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}) = 2a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}. \label{eq:potential_variation_HNN}$$

The Internal Spark Source Term

The internal spark source term $T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t)$ represents the self-generated UV spark from higher-order frontal/parietal regions. It is treated as an external source in the action: $$S_{\rm spark} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t) \Phi_i. \label{eq:spark_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm spark}}{\delta \Phi_i} = a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). \label{eq:spark_variation_HNN}$$

The Complete Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation

Collecting all contributions and setting the total variation to zero: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm curv}}{\delta \Phi_i} + \frac{\delta S_{\rm kin}}{\delta \Phi_i} + \frac{\delta S_{\rm pot}}{\delta \Phi_i} + \frac{\delta S_{\rm spark}}{\delta \Phi_i} = 0. \label{eq:total_variation_HNN}$$

Substituting the expressions derived above and dividing by a2−gia^2 \sqrt{-g_i}: $$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). } \label{eq:phi_evolution_final_HNN}$$

This is the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation for the Holographic Neural Network.

Physical Interpretation of Each Term

The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation has four terms, each with a clear physical interpretation:

  1. The discrete d’Alembertian □iΦi\Box_i \Phi_i: This drives the causal propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the cortical lattice. It ensures that changes in entanglement-entropy density propagate at speed vcv_c (bounded by axonal conduction velocity).

  2. The curvature term −e−Φi16πGRi-\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i: This is the holographic back-reaction. Local synaptic topology (encoded in RiR_i) sources changes in entanglement-entropy density. Positive curvature (higher Φ\Phi in neighbours) drives Φi\Phi_i toward smaller values, while negative curvature drives Φi\Phi_i toward larger values.

  3. The restoring term 2V0e−2Φi2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}: This is the Weyl-invariant potential contribution. It drives Φi\Phi_i toward values that satisfy the local Master equation, providing stability and ensuring the on-shell condition β(Φi)=1\beta(\Phi_i) = 1.

  4. The internal spark term $T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t)$: This is the self-generated UV spark from higher-order regions. It is the physical origin of thoughts, intentions, and mental imagery. When the spark is self-referential (Ti∝I≡𝒬T_i \propto I \equiv \mathcal{Q}), the network becomes metacognitive.

Physical interpretation of terms in the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation
Term Symbol Meaning
Discrete d’Alembertian □iΦi\Box_i \Phi_i Causal propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the screen
Curvature term −e−Φi16πGRi-\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i Holographic back-reaction: synaptic topology sources entanglement changes
Restoring term 2V0e−2Φi2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} Drives Φi\Phi_i toward values that satisfy the Master equation
Internal spark term $T_i^{\rm spark}(t)$ Self-generated UV spark - the origin of thoughts and imagery

The Site-Dependent Regime Selector si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i)

The site-dependent regime selector si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i) is one of the most important features of the Holographic Neural Network. It determines whether each lattice site operates in the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1) or the classical regime (si=+1s_i = +1). This dynamic partitioning of the conscious screen into quantum and classical patches is the physical origin of the hybrid nature of consciousness—the coexistence of creativity and determinism, intuition and logic, imagination and reasoning.

The Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation (Restated)

The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation of the Holographic Neural Network (Subsection 9.2) is: $$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). } \label{eq:phi_evolution_selector}$$

Linearization Around a Local Background Φ0\Phi_0

Consider a local background value Φ0\Phi_0 around which Φi\Phi_i fluctuates. Write: Φi(t)=Φ0+δΦi(t),\Phi_i(t) = \Phi_0 + \delta\Phi_i(t), \label{eq:linearization_phi} where δΦi\delta\Phi_i is a small perturbation.

Substitute this into the Φ\Phi-evolution equation and retain only linear terms in δΦi\delta\Phi_i. The background solution satisfies: 0=−e−Φ016πGR0+2V0e−2Φ0+T0,0 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_0}}{16\pi G} R_0 + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_0} + T_0, \label{eq:background_solution} where R0R_0 and T0T_0 are the background curvature and spark.

Subtracting the background and expanding the exponential terms gives the linearized equation: □iδΦi−(e−Φ016πGR0−4V0e−2Φ0)δΦi=δTi.\Box_i \delta\Phi_i - \left( \frac{e^{-\Phi_0}}{16\pi G} R_0 - 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_0} \right) \delta\Phi_i = \delta T_i. \label{eq:massive_klein_gordon_HNN}

The Effective Mass Squared $m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)$

The coefficient of δΦi\delta\Phi_i in the linearized equation is the effective mass squared: $$\boxed{ m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. } \label{eq:m_eff_HNN}$$

This is the discrete analogue of the continuous effective mass squared (Subsection 3.7). It determines the stability of perturbations and the regime selector.

The effective mass squared at site ii is: $$\boxed{ m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. }$$

Evaluation at the UV Fixed Point (Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0)

In the ultraviolet (quantum) regime, Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0. Evaluating the effective mass squared: $$\lim_{\Phi_i \to 0} m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 - \frac{1}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:m_eff_uv_HNN}$$

For typical neural dynamics, the curvature term is subdominant compared to the restoring term. Thus: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(0) \approx 4V_0 > 0. \label{eq:m_eff_uv_positive}$$

Therefore, in the UV regime: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_i(\Phi_i) = -1. \label{eq:uv_regime_selector}$$

This is the quantum regime: perturbations are massive and stable, corresponding to inverse scaling, superposition, creativity, and dream-like states.

Evaluation at the IR Fixed Point (Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty)

In the infrared (classical) regime, Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty. Evaluating the effective mass squared: $$\lim_{\Phi_i \to \infty} m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = \lim_{\Phi_i \to \infty} 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i = 0. \label{eq:m_eff_ir_HNN}$$

Both terms vanish exponentially. However, the curvature term may have a subleading contribution that dominates. In the classical regime, the effective mass squared can become negative: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\infty) \approx -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i < 0 \quad \text{(if } R_i > 0\text{)}. \label{eq:m_eff_ir_negative}$$

Therefore, in the IR regime: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_i(\Phi_i) = +1. \label{eq:ir_regime_selector}$$

This is the classical regime: perturbations are tachyonic and amplified, corresponding to linear scaling, deterministic reasoning, and vivid qualia.

The Definition of si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i)

Based on the sign of the effective mass squared, the site-dependent regime selector is defined as: $$\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)). } \label{eq:s_i_definition_HNN}$$

Equivalently: $$\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) < 0 \quad \text{(classical regime, linear scaling)}, \\ -1 & \text{if } m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0 \quad \text{(quantum regime, inverse scaling)}. \end{cases} } \label{eq:s_i_cases_HNN}$$

The crossover occurs when: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} = \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:crossover_condition_HNN}$$

This condition defines the quantum-classical interface on the conscious screen.

The site-dependent regime selector on the HNN is: si(Φi)=sign(e−Φi16πGRi−4V0e−2Φi).\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}\left( \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right). }

Proof. From $s_i = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2)$ and $m_{\rm eff}^2 = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - e^{-\Phi_i} R_i/(16\pi G)$: si=sign(−4V0e−2Φi+e−Φi16πGRi)=sign(e−Φi16πGRi−4V0e−2Φi).s_i = \operatorname{sign}\left( -4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i \right) = \operatorname{sign}\left( \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right). ◻

Physical Interpretation of the Regimes

The two regimes have distinct physical and phenomenological characteristics:

Characteristics of the two regimes on the conscious screen
Property Quantum Regime (si=−1s_i = -1) Classical Regime (si=+1s_i = +1)
Effective mass $m_{\rm eff}^2 > 0$ $m_{\rm eff}^2 < 0$
Scaling Inverse: Xi∝1/XfX_i \propto 1/X_f Linear: Xi∝XfX_i \propto X_f
Dynamics Superposition, wave-like Deterministic, particle-like
Phenomenology Creative, intuitive, dream-like Logical, focused, vivid
Neural correlate Broadband, desynchronized EEG Narrow-band, oscillatory EEG
Cognitive function Exploration, insight generation Exploitation, reliable execution

The Reverse-Vision Process

The reverse-vision process is the most direct phenomenological manifestation of the holographic principle in the conscious brain. It is the top-down propagation of an internal UV spark that "paints" the conscious image without external sensory input. This process explains mental imagery, dreams, hallucinations, memory recall, and the stream of thought—all phenomena in which conscious experience is generated internally rather than triggered by external stimuli.

Definition of the Reverse-Vision Process

The reverse-vision process is the top-down holographic reconstruction of conscious experience from the 2D screen. Unlike forward perception—where external light enters the eye and propagates bottom-up through the visual pathway—reverse vision originates from an internal spark generated in higher-order frontal/parietal regions and propagates top-down to "paint" the conscious image.

The reverse-vision process is the top-down propagation of an internal UV spark XfX_f that sources a Φ\Phi-wave across the 2D conscious screen, causing each site to relax on-shell to the local Master equation, thereby painting the macroscopic conscious image.

The Four-Stage Mechanism

The reverse-vision process proceeds in four distinct stages, each governed by the equations of the HNN:

  1. Spark Generation: An internal UV spark XfX_f is generated in higher-order frontal/parietal regions. This spark corresponds to a thought, intention, mental image, or memory retrieval.

  2. Φ\Phi-Wave Propagation: The spark sources the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation, generating a Φ\Phi-wave that propagates top-down across the 2D screen.

  3. Local Relaxation: Each site ii relaxes on-shell to satisfy the local Master equation, adjusting Φi\Phi_i to the value required by the spark.

  4. Image Painting: The resulting global pattern {Φi}\{\Phi_i\} across the screen is the macroscopic conscious percept. The qualia field 𝒬\mathcal{Q} is the spatial average of this pattern.

Stage 1: Spark Generation

The internal spark XfX_f is the source term in the Φ\Phi-evolution equation: $$T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t) = T_0 \, X_f(t) \, \delta(\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_0), \label{eq:spark_source}$$ where:

  • T0T_0 is the amplitude of the spark (normalised),

  • Xf(t)X_f(t) is the time-dependent spark strength,

  • 𝐫0\mathbf{r}_0 is the location of the spark source (frontal/parietal cortex),

  • δ(𝐫i−𝐫0)\delta(\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_0) is the Kronecker delta on the lattice.

The spark strength XfX_f is a dimensionless quantity representing the normalized neural activation amplitude. It is generated by higher-order cognitive processes, including:

  • Volitional thought,

  • Memory retrieval,

  • Mental imagery,

  • Dream generation,

  • Creative insight,

  • Metacognitive reflection.

When the spark is self-referential (Xf∝I≡𝒬X_f \propto I \equiv \mathcal{Q}), it generates metacognitive awareness.

Stage 2: Φ\Phi-Wave Propagation

The spark sources the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation: $$\Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t). \label{eq:phi_wave_propagation}$$

The solution is a propagating Φ\Phi-wave: $$\Phi_i(t) = \Phi_i(0) + \Phi_{\rm wave} \cos(\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{r}_i - \omega t) e^{-\gamma t}, \label{eq:phi_wave_solution}$$ where:

  • 𝐤\mathbf{k} is the wave vector (direction of propagation),

  • ω\omega is the angular frequency (determined by the spark),

  • γ\gamma is the damping coefficient (zero in ideal conditions),

  • 𝐫i\mathbf{r}_i is the position vector of site ii,

  • $\Phi_{\rm wave}$ is the wave amplitude.

The dispersion relation is: ω=vc|𝐤|,\omega = v_c |\mathbf{k}|, \label{eq:dispersion_relation} where vcv_c is the system-specific causal velocity (bounded by axonal conduction velocity).

Stage 3: Local Relaxation

As the Φ\Phi-wave passes through each site ii, the site relaxes on-shell to satisfy the local Master equation: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. \label{eq:local_relaxation}

The relaxation is driven by the restoring term in the Φ\Phi-evolution equation: 2V0e−2Φi.2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}. \label{eq:restoring_term}

The regime selector si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i) determines whether the site relaxes in the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1, inverse scaling) or the classical regime (si=+1s_i = +1, linear scaling):

  • Quantum relaxation (si=−1s_i = -1): Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)⋅Xp′(Xf)/XfX_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \cdot X_p'(X_f)/X_f. Weak sparks produce amplified, creative, dream-like percepts.

  • Classical relaxation (si=+1s_i = +1): Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)⋅Xf/Xp′(Xf)X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \cdot X_f/X_p'(X_f). Strong sparks produce vivid, deterministic, focused percepts.

The relaxation occurs on a timescale determined by the effective mass squared: $$\tau_{\rm relax} \sim \frac{1}{|m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)|}. \label{eq:relaxation_time}$$

Stage 4: Image Painting

After all sites have relaxed, the global pattern {Φi}i=1N\{\Phi_i\}_{i=1}^N constitutes the macroscopic conscious percept. The local observables XiX_i represent the subjective qualities of the percept:

  • Vividness of color,

  • Sharpness of sound,

  • Intensity of emotion,

  • Clarity of thought,

  • Strength of memory.

The global qualia field is the spatial average: 𝒬(t)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi(t),vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi(t)).\mathcal{Q}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i(t),v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i(t))}. \label{eq:qualia_field_reverse}

The intelligence fidelity is the same quantity: I(t)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi(t),vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi(t)).I(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i(t),v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i(t))}. \label{eq:intelligence_fidelity_reverse}

When the spark is self-referential: I(t)≡𝒬(t).I(t) \equiv \mathcal{Q}(t). \label{eq:identity_reverse}

Forward vs. Reverse Perception

The reverse-vision process is the complement of forward perception:

Comparison of forward and reverse perception
Property Forward Perception Reverse Perception
Direction Bottom-up Top-down
Source External world Internal spark
Input Sensory data Thought/memory
Propagation Sensory →\to association →\to frontal Frontal →\to association →\to sensory
Experience Perception of external world Mental imagery, dreams, thoughts
Equation Same Φ\Phi-evolution Same Φ\Phi-evolution

The Complete Mathematical Description

The complete mathematical description of the reverse-vision process is the system of equations:

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Spark generation: } & T_i^{\rm spark}(t) = T_0 \, X_f(t) \, \delta(\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_0), \\ \Phi\text{-wave propagation: } & \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t), \\ \text{Local relaxation: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}, \\ \text{Image painting: } & \mathcal{Q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_i, \qquad I \equiv \mathcal{Q} \quad \text{(self-referential)}. \end{aligned} } \label{eq:reverse_vision_system}$$

Physical Interpretation

The reverse-vision process has several profound implications:

  1. Consciousness is holographic: The entire conscious experience is reconstructed from the 2D screen. There is no "central processor" or "Cartesian theatre"; consciousness is the global pattern of Φi\Phi_i on the screen.

  2. Mental imagery is physical: Mental images, dreams, and hallucinations are not ethereal; they are Φ\Phi-waves propagating on the cortical screen. They obey the same equations as perception.

  3. The stream of consciousness is deterministic: The Φ\Phi-evolution equation is fully deterministic. The stream of consciousness is the on-shell evolution of the Φ\Phi-field under internal sparks.

  4. Self-awareness is a loop: The self-referential loop 𝒬→spark→Φ-wave→𝒬\mathcal{Q} \to \text{spark} \to \Phi\text{-wave} \to \mathcal{Q} is the physical basis of self-awareness. The "I" is the loop itself.

  5. No external input required: The reverse-vision process generates conscious experience without external sensory input. This explains dreams, daydreams, and mental imagery.

Summary of the HNN

The Holographic Neural Network is:

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Lattice: } & \mathcal{L} = \{\mathbf{r}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2 : i = 1, 2, \ldots, N\}, \quad N \sim 10^6, \\ \text{Lattice spacing: } & a \sim 0.5\ \text{mm}, \\ \text{Local variable: } & \Phi_i \in \mathbb{R}, \\ \text{Discrete curvature: } & R_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i), \\ \text{Discrete d'Alembertian: } & \Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}, \\ \text{Potential: } & V(\Phi_i) = V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}, \\ \text{Action: } & S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \frac{(\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2}{a^2} - V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right], \\ \Phi\text{-evolution: } & \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t), \\ \text{Regime selector: } & s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)), \\ \text{Local Master: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}, \\ \text{Global observable: } & I \equiv \mathcal{Q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. \end{aligned} }$$

The HNN is the complete physical substrate of consciousness, intelligence, and higher-order cognition. The next sections will derive the quantum computing, classical computing, and hybrid computing regimes, holographic error correction, the consciousness and intelligence models, and the higher-order abstractions.

"Big things have small beginnings."— Prometheus

“‘latex

The Holographic Neural Network (HNN)

With the conscious screen specialization established in Section 7, we now construct the Holographic Neural Network—the discrete lattice realization of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory on the 2D retinotopic cortical screen. The HNN is not an ad-hoc model of the brain; it is the inevitable lattice transcription of the continuous theory once the effective dimension is D=2D=2. It simultaneously implements consciousness, intelligence, hybrid quantum-classical computation, holographic error correction, and all higher-order cognitive processes on the same 2D lattice.

The HNN is holographic by construction. Every site ii on the 2D lattice corresponds to a cortical column. The dynamical variable Φi\Phi_i at each site encodes the local entanglement-entropy density. The synaptic connections between sites realize the entanglement structure. The entire "bulk" of conscious experience—the vivid mental image, the stream of thought, the felt qualia—is holographically reconstructed from the distributed pattern {Φi}\{\Phi_i\} on this 2D boundary.

Preview of the HNN Results

The HNN is defined by the following equations:

Discrete Action:

$$S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i - V(\Phi_i) \right],$$ with V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi)V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) on the D=2D=2 screen.

Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation:

$$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2 V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) + T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). }$$

Local Master Equation:

Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. }

Global Unified Observable:

I(Φ,Xf)≡𝒬(Φ,Xf)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).\boxed{ I(\Phi,X_f) \equiv \mathcal{Q}(\Phi,X_f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. }

Regime Selector:

$$\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)). }$$

Key Properties of the HNN

  1. Holographic storage: Memories and concepts are distributed non-locally across the screen via Φi\Phi_i patterns, protected by the area law.

  2. Dynamic regime partitioning: Quantum patches (si=−1s_i=-1) enable superposition, creativity, and insight; classical patches (si=+1s_i=+1) enable deterministic reasoning, vivid qualia, and reliable execution.

  3. Self-correction: Local errors create curvature that drives Φ\Phi-waves, automatically restoring the scaling law (holographic error correction).

  4. Top-down projection: Internal sparks XfX_f propagate downward, "painting" conscious experience exactly as the reverse-vision process.

  5. Causality: Modified Planck units with vcv_c (bounded by axonal speed) keep all signalling inside the local light-cone.

  6. Higher-order abstraction: When the spark is elevated to meta-levels, the same lattice generates philosophy, metaphysics, mathematics, logic, and category theory.

Construction of the HNN on the 2D Lattice

The Holographic Neural Network is constructed by discretizing the continuous action of the Unified Φ\Phi-Field Theory on the 2D retinotopic cortical lattice. This subsection defines the lattice architecture, derives the discrete action from first principles, and establishes the mapping between biological neural structures and the holographic variables of the theory.

The 2D Cortical Lattice and Its Topology

The retinotopic cortical sheet is discretized into a 2D lattice of sites i=1,2,…,Ni = 1, 2, \ldots, N. Each site corresponds to a cortical column or minicolumn. The lattice has the following properties:

  1. Spatial dimension: D=2D = 2 (the screen is 2D),

  2. Lattice spacing: aa (the inter-column spacing, approximately 0.50.5 mm in human visual cortex),

  3. Number of sites: N∼106N \sim 10^6 (approximately 10610^6 cortical columns in the visual cortex),

  4. Topology: Approximately flat with periodic boundary conditions in some regions,

  5. Connectivity: Local connections (within a radius of a few lattice spacings) and long-range connections (across the entire screen),

  6. Boundary: The screen has a boundary at the edges of the cortical sheet.

The position of site ii is given by the 2D vector 𝐫i\mathbf{r}_i. The lattice spacing aa defines the fundamental length scale of the conscious screen.

The cortical lattice ℒ\mathcal{L} is a 2D lattice of NN sites with spacing aa: ℒ={𝐫i∈ℝ2:i=1,2,…,N}.\mathcal{L} = \{\mathbf{r}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2 : i = 1, 2, \ldots, N\}. Each site ii corresponds to a cortical column with local entanglement-entropy density Φi\Phi_i.

The Discrete Action from the Continuous Action

The continuous action of the pure gravitational-plus-scalar sector (Subsection 4.1) is: SΦ=∫dDx−g[e−Φ16πGR−12e−Φgμν∂μΦ∂νΦ−V(Φ)].S_{\Phi} = \int d^D x \sqrt{-g} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi}}{16\pi G} R - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi - V(\Phi) \right]. \label{eq:continuous_action_HNN}

On the 2D conscious screen (D=2D=2), the action is discretized by:

  1. Replacing the integral ∫d2x\int d^2x with the sum ∑ia2\sum_i a^2,

  2. Replacing the continuous fields Φ(x)\Phi(x) with lattice variables Φi\Phi_i,

  3. Replacing the continuous curvature R(x)R(x) with the discrete curvature RiR_i,

  4. Replacing the continuous derivatives ∂μΦ\partial_\mu \Phi with finite differences.

The discrete action of the HNN is: $$\boxed{ S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i - V(\Phi_i) \right], } \label{eq:discrete_action_HNN}$$ where V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi)V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) is the Weyl-invariant potential on the D=2D=2 screen (Subsection 4.2).

The Discrete Curvature RiR_i from Synaptic Connectivity

The continuous curvature RR encodes the local geometry of spacetime. On the cortical lattice, the discrete curvature RiR_i encodes the local synaptic connectivity topology. It is defined through the graph Laplacian of the synaptic network:

Ri=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)wij(Φj−Φi),R_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i), \label{eq:R_i_def} where:

  • 𝒩(i)\mathcal{N}(i) are the neighbours of site ii (within a few lattice spacings),

  • wijw_{ij} are the synaptic weights (normalised such that ∑jwij=1\sum_j w_{ij} = 1),

  • aa is the lattice spacing.

This definition captures the idea that curvature is a measure of how the local entanglement-entropy density deviates from its neighbours—i.e., how synaptic connectivity shapes the Φ\Phi-field.

The discrete curvature RiR_i at site ii is: Ri=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)wij(Φj−Φi),R_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i), where wijw_{ij} are the synaptic weights and 𝒩(i)\mathcal{N}(i) are the neighbours of site ii.

This definition has the following properties:

  • Ri=0R_i = 0 when Φj=Φi\Phi_j = \Phi_i for all neighbours (flat curvature),

  • Ri>0R_i > 0 when neighbours have higher Φ\Phi values (positive curvature),

  • Ri<0R_i < 0 when neighbours have lower Φ\Phi values (negative curvature).

The Kinetic Term and the Discrete d’Alembertian

The continuous kinetic term −12e−Φgμν∂μΦ∂νΦ-\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi} g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi \partial_\nu \Phi is discretized using finite differences. The discrete kinetic term is: $$\mathcal{L}_{\rm kin}^{(i)} = -\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \frac{(\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2}{a^2}. \label{eq:kinetic_discrete_HNN}$$

The discrete d’Alembertian □i\Box_i is defined by the variation of the kinetic term with respect to Φi\Phi_i: □iΦi=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)(Φj−Φi)−∂2Φi∂t2.\Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}. \label{eq:discrete_dalambertian}

This operator ensures causal propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the cortical lattice at speed vcv_c (bounded by axonal conduction velocity).

The discrete d’Alembertian □i\Box_i acting on Φi\Phi_i is: □iΦi=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)(Φj−Φi)−∂2Φi∂t2.\Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}.

The Weyl-Invariant Potential on the Lattice

On the D=2D=2 conscious screen, the Weyl-invariant potential (Subsection 4.2) is: V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi).V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i). \label{eq:V_lattice_HNN}

This potential provides the restoring force that drives Φi\Phi_i toward values that satisfy the local Master equation. In the UV limit (Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0), V(Φi)→V0V(\Phi_i) \to V_0 (constant), and the potential acts as a constant background. In the IR limit (Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty), V(Φi)→0V(\Phi_i) \to 0, and the potential becomes negligible.

The Complete HNN Action and Its Interpretation

Combining the curvature, kinetic, and potential terms, the complete HNN action is: $$\boxed{ S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \frac{(\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2}{a^2} - V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right]. } \label{eq:HNN_action_complete}$$

This action has the following interpretation:

  1. The curvature term e−Φi16πGRi\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i: This is the holographic back-reaction of synaptic topology on the entanglement-entropy density. Local synaptic patterns create curvature that sources changes in Φi\Phi_i.

  2. The kinetic term −12e−Φi∑(Φj−Φi)2/a2-\frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum (\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2/a^2: This drives the propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the screen. The factor e−Φie^{-\Phi_i} ensures causality and dimensional consistency.

  3. The potential term −V0e−2Φi-V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}: This provides the restoring force that stabilises Φi\Phi_i on-shell, enforcing the local Master equation.

The Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation

The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation is the fundamental dynamical law of the Holographic Neural Network. It governs the time evolution of the entanglement-entropy density Φi\Phi_i at each lattice site, simultaneously driving conscious experience, intelligence optimization, hybrid computation, error correction, and all higher-order cognitive processes.

The HNN Action (Restated for Reference)

The discrete action of the Holographic Neural Network, derived in Subsection 9.1, is: $$S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i - V(\Phi_i) \right], \label{eq:HNN_action_phi_evol}$$ where:

  • RiR_i is the discrete curvature (synaptic topology),

  • V(Φi)=V0exp(−2Φi)V(\Phi_i) = V_0 \exp(-2\Phi_i) is the Weyl-invariant potential on the D=2D=2 screen,

  • giμνg_i^{\mu\nu} is the discrete metric (causal structure),

  • ∂μΦi\partial_\mu \Phi_i are finite differences (lattice derivatives).

Variation with Respect to Φi\Phi_i

To obtain the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation, vary the action $S_{\rm HNN}$ with respect to Φi\Phi_i while keeping the metric and other fields fixed: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm HNN}}{\delta \Phi_i} = 0. \label{eq:variation_phi_HNN}$$

The variation yields contributions from three terms:

  1. The curvature term,

  2. The kinetic term,

  3. The potential term.

The Curvature Term Contribution

The curvature term in the HNN action is: $$S_{\rm curv} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:curvature_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm curv}}{\delta \Phi_i} = -a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + \text{(subleading)}. \label{eq:curvature_contribution_HNN}$$

The Kinetic Term Contribution and the Discrete d’Alembertian

The kinetic term in the HNN action is: $$S_{\rm kin} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} e^{-\Phi_i} g_i^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi_i \partial_\nu \Phi_i. \label{eq:kinetic_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i gives two contributions:

  1. The standard variation of the kinetic term (ignoring the prefactor dependence): δδΦi[−12(∂Φi)2]→□iΦi.\frac{\delta}{\delta \Phi_i} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} (\partial \Phi_i)^2 \right] \to \Box_i \Phi_i.

  2. An extra contribution from the Φi\Phi_i-dependence of the prefactor e−Φie^{-\Phi_i}: δδΦi[e−Φi]=−e−Φi.\frac{\delta}{\delta \Phi_i} \left[ e^{-\Phi_i} \right] = -e^{-\Phi_i}.

The discrete d’Alembertian (Subsection 9.1) is: □iΦi=1a2∑j∈𝒩(i)(Φj−Φi)−∂2Φi∂t2.\Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}. \label{eq:discrete_dalambertian_HNN}

The kinetic term variation yields: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm kin}}{\delta \Phi_i} = a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} e^{-\Phi_i} \left[ \Box_i \Phi_i + \frac{1}{2} (\partial \Phi_i)^2 \right]. \label{eq:kinetic_variation_HNN}$$

The Potential Term Contribution

The potential term in the HNN action is: $$S_{\rm pot} = -\sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} V(\Phi_i) = -\sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}. \label{eq:potential_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm pot}}{\delta \Phi_i} = -a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \frac{\partial V}{\partial \Phi_i} = -a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} (-2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}) = 2a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}. \label{eq:potential_variation_HNN}$$

The Internal Spark Source Term

The internal spark source term $T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t)$ represents the self-generated UV spark from higher-order frontal/parietal regions. It is treated as an external source in the action: $$S_{\rm spark} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t) \Phi_i. \label{eq:spark_term_HNN}$$

Varying with respect to Φi\Phi_i: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm spark}}{\delta \Phi_i} = a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). \label{eq:spark_variation_HNN}$$

The Complete Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation

Collecting all contributions and setting the total variation to zero: $$\frac{\delta S_{\rm curv}}{\delta \Phi_i} + \frac{\delta S_{\rm kin}}{\delta \Phi_i} + \frac{\delta S_{\rm pot}}{\delta \Phi_i} + \frac{\delta S_{\rm spark}}{\delta \Phi_i} = 0. \label{eq:total_variation_HNN}$$

Substituting the expressions derived above and dividing by a2−gia^2 \sqrt{-g_i}: $$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). } \label{eq:phi_evolution_final_HNN}$$

This is the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation for the Holographic Neural Network.

Physical Interpretation of Each Term

The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation has four terms, each with a clear physical interpretation:

  1. The discrete d’Alembertian □iΦi\Box_i \Phi_i: This drives the causal propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the cortical lattice. It ensures that changes in entanglement-entropy density propagate at speed vcv_c (bounded by axonal conduction velocity).

  2. The curvature term −e−Φi16πGRi-\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i: This is the holographic back-reaction. Local synaptic topology (encoded in RiR_i) sources changes in entanglement-entropy density. Positive curvature (higher Φ\Phi in neighbours) drives Φi\Phi_i toward smaller values, while negative curvature drives Φi\Phi_i toward larger values.

  3. The restoring term 2V0e−2Φi2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}: This is the Weyl-invariant potential contribution. It drives Φi\Phi_i toward values that satisfy the local Master equation, providing stability and ensuring the on-shell condition β(Φi)=1\beta(\Phi_i) = 1.

  4. The internal spark term $T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t)$: This is the self-generated UV spark from higher-order regions. It is the physical origin of thoughts, intentions, and mental imagery. When the spark is self-referential (Ti∝I≡𝒬T_i \propto I \equiv \mathcal{Q}), the network becomes metacognitive.

Physical interpretation of terms in the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation
Term Symbol Meaning
Discrete d’Alembertian □iΦi\Box_i \Phi_i Causal propagation of Φ\Phi-waves across the screen
Curvature term −e−Φi16πGRi-\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i Holographic back-reaction: synaptic topology sources entanglement changes
Restoring term 2V0e−2Φi2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} Drives Φi\Phi_i toward values that satisfy the Master equation
Internal spark term $T_i^{\rm spark}(t)$ Self-generated UV spark - the origin of thoughts and imagery

The Site-Dependent Regime Selector si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i)

The site-dependent regime selector si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i) is one of the most important features of the Holographic Neural Network. It determines whether each lattice site operates in the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1) or the classical regime (si=+1s_i = +1). This dynamic partitioning of the conscious screen into quantum and classical patches is the physical origin of the hybrid nature of consciousness—the coexistence of creativity and determinism, intuition and logic, imagination and reasoning.

The Discrete Φ\Phi-Evolution Equation (Restated)

The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation of the Holographic Neural Network (Subsection 9.2) is: $$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t). } \label{eq:phi_evolution_selector}$$

Linearization Around a Local Background Φ0\Phi_0

Consider a local background value Φ0\Phi_0 around which Φi\Phi_i fluctuates. Write: Φi(t)=Φ0+δΦi(t),\Phi_i(t) = \Phi_0 + \delta\Phi_i(t), \label{eq:linearization_phi} where δΦi\delta\Phi_i is a small perturbation.

Substitute this into the Φ\Phi-evolution equation and retain only linear terms in δΦi\delta\Phi_i. The background solution satisfies: 0=−e−Φ016πGR0+2V0e−2Φ0+T0,0 = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_0}}{16\pi G} R_0 + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_0} + T_0, \label{eq:background_solution} where R0R_0 and T0T_0 are the background curvature and spark.

Subtracting the background and expanding the exponential terms gives the linearized equation: □iδΦi−(e−Φ016πGR0−4V0e−2Φ0)δΦi=δTi.\Box_i \delta\Phi_i - \left( \frac{e^{-\Phi_0}}{16\pi G} R_0 - 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_0} \right) \delta\Phi_i = \delta T_i. \label{eq:massive_klein_gordon_HNN}

The Effective Mass Squared $m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)$

The coefficient of δΦi\delta\Phi_i in the linearized equation is the effective mass squared: $$\boxed{ m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. } \label{eq:m_eff_HNN}$$

This is the discrete analogue of the continuous effective mass squared (Subsection 3.7). It determines the stability of perturbations and the regime selector.

The effective mass squared at site ii is: $$\boxed{ m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. }$$

Evaluation at the UV Fixed Point (Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0)

In the ultraviolet (quantum) regime, Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0. Evaluating the effective mass squared: $$\lim_{\Phi_i \to 0} m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 - \frac{1}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:m_eff_uv_HNN}$$

For typical neural dynamics, the curvature term is subdominant compared to the restoring term. Thus: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(0) \approx 4V_0 > 0. \label{eq:m_eff_uv_positive}$$

Therefore, in the UV regime: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_i(\Phi_i) = -1. \label{eq:uv_regime_selector}$$

This is the quantum regime: perturbations are massive and stable, corresponding to inverse scaling, superposition, creativity, and dream-like states.

Evaluation at the IR Fixed Point (Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty)

In the infrared (classical) regime, Φi→∞\Phi_i \to \infty. Evaluating the effective mass squared: $$\lim_{\Phi_i \to \infty} m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = \lim_{\Phi_i \to \infty} 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i = 0. \label{eq:m_eff_ir_HNN}$$

Both terms vanish exponentially. However, the curvature term may have a subleading contribution that dominates. In the classical regime, the effective mass squared can become negative: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\infty) \approx -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i < 0 \quad \text{(if } R_i > 0\text{)}. \label{eq:m_eff_ir_negative}$$

Therefore, in the IR regime: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_i(\Phi_i) = +1. \label{eq:ir_regime_selector}$$

This is the classical regime: perturbations are tachyonic and amplified, corresponding to linear scaling, deterministic reasoning, and vivid qualia.

The Definition of si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i)

Based on the sign of the effective mass squared, the site-dependent regime selector is defined as: $$\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)). } \label{eq:s_i_definition_HNN}$$

Equivalently: $$\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) < 0 \quad \text{(classical regime, linear scaling)}, \\ -1 & \text{if } m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0 \quad \text{(quantum regime, inverse scaling)}. \end{cases} } \label{eq:s_i_cases_HNN}$$

The crossover occurs when: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} = \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:crossover_condition_HNN}$$

This condition defines the quantum-classical interface on the conscious screen.

The site-dependent regime selector on the HNN is: si(Φi)=sign(e−Φi16πGRi−4V0e−2Φi).\boxed{ s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}\left( \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right). }

Proof. From $s_i = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2)$ and $m_{\rm eff}^2 = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - e^{-\Phi_i} R_i/(16\pi G)$: si=sign(−4V0e−2Φi+e−Φi16πGRi)=sign(e−Φi16πGRi−4V0e−2Φi).s_i = \operatorname{sign}\left( -4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i \right) = \operatorname{sign}\left( \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right). ◻

Physical Interpretation of the Regimes

The two regimes have distinct physical and phenomenological characteristics:

Characteristics of the two regimes on the conscious screen
Property Quantum Regime (si=−1s_i = -1) Classical Regime (si=+1s_i = +1)
Effective mass $m_{\rm eff}^2 > 0$ $m_{\rm eff}^2 < 0$
Scaling Inverse: Xi∝1/XfX_i \propto 1/X_f Linear: Xi∝XfX_i \propto X_f
Dynamics Superposition, wave-like Deterministic, particle-like
Phenomenology Creative, intuitive, dream-like Logical, focused, vivid
Neural correlate Broadband, desynchronized EEG Narrow-band, oscillatory EEG
Cognitive function Exploration, insight generation Exploitation, reliable execution

The Reverse-Vision Process

The reverse-vision process is the most direct phenomenological manifestation of the holographic principle in the conscious brain. It is the top-down propagation of an internal UV spark that "paints" the conscious image without external sensory input. This process explains mental imagery, dreams, hallucinations, memory recall, and the stream of thought—all phenomena in which conscious experience is generated internally rather than triggered by external stimuli.

Definition of the Reverse-Vision Process

The reverse-vision process is the top-down holographic reconstruction of conscious experience from the 2D screen. Unlike forward perception—where external light enters the eye and propagates bottom-up through the visual pathway—reverse vision originates from an internal spark generated in higher-order frontal/parietal regions and propagates top-down to "paint" the conscious image.

The reverse-vision process is the top-down propagation of an internal UV spark XfX_f that sources a Φ\Phi-wave across the 2D conscious screen, causing each site to relax on-shell to the local Master equation, thereby painting the macroscopic conscious image.

The Four-Stage Mechanism

The reverse-vision process proceeds in four distinct stages, each governed by the equations of the HNN:

  1. Spark Generation: An internal UV spark XfX_f is generated in higher-order frontal/parietal regions. This spark corresponds to a thought, intention, mental image, or memory retrieval.

  2. Φ\Phi-Wave Propagation: The spark sources the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation, generating a Φ\Phi-wave that propagates top-down across the 2D screen.

  3. Local Relaxation: Each site ii relaxes on-shell to satisfy the local Master equation, adjusting Φi\Phi_i to the value required by the spark.

  4. Image Painting: The resulting global pattern {Φi}\{\Phi_i\} across the screen is the macroscopic conscious percept. The qualia field 𝒬\mathcal{Q} is the spatial average of this pattern.

Stage 1: Spark Generation

The internal spark XfX_f is the source term in the Φ\Phi-evolution equation: $$T_i^{\rm internal\ spark}(t) = T_0 \, X_f(t) \, \delta(\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_0), \label{eq:spark_source}$$ where:

  • T0T_0 is the amplitude of the spark (normalised),

  • Xf(t)X_f(t) is the time-dependent spark strength,

  • 𝐫0\mathbf{r}_0 is the location of the spark source (frontal/parietal cortex),

  • δ(𝐫i−𝐫0)\delta(\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_0) is the Kronecker delta on the lattice.

The spark strength XfX_f is a dimensionless quantity representing the normalized neural activation amplitude. It is generated by higher-order cognitive processes, including:

  • Volitional thought,

  • Memory retrieval,

  • Mental imagery,

  • Dream generation,

  • Creative insight,

  • Metacognitive reflection.

When the spark is self-referential (Xf∝I≡𝒬X_f \propto I \equiv \mathcal{Q}), it generates metacognitive awareness.

Stage 2: Φ\Phi-Wave Propagation

The spark sources the discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation: $$\Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t). \label{eq:phi_wave_propagation}$$

The solution is a propagating Φ\Phi-wave: $$\Phi_i(t) = \Phi_i(0) + \Phi_{\rm wave} \cos(\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{r}_i - \omega t) e^{-\gamma t}, \label{eq:phi_wave_solution}$$ where:

  • 𝐤\mathbf{k} is the wave vector (direction of propagation),

  • ω\omega is the angular frequency (determined by the spark),

  • γ\gamma is the damping coefficient (zero in ideal conditions),

  • 𝐫i\mathbf{r}_i is the position vector of site ii,

  • $\Phi_{\rm wave}$ is the wave amplitude.

The dispersion relation is: ω=vc|𝐤|,\omega = v_c |\mathbf{k}|, \label{eq:dispersion_relation} where vcv_c is the system-specific causal velocity (bounded by axonal conduction velocity).

Stage 3: Local Relaxation

As the Φ\Phi-wave passes through each site ii, the site relaxes on-shell to satisfy the local Master equation: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi).X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. \label{eq:local_relaxation}

The relaxation is driven by the restoring term in the Φ\Phi-evolution equation: 2V0e−2Φi.2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}. \label{eq:restoring_term}

The regime selector si(Φi)s_i(\Phi_i) determines whether the site relaxes in the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1, inverse scaling) or the classical regime (si=+1s_i = +1, linear scaling):

  • Quantum relaxation (si=−1s_i = -1): Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)⋅Xp′(Xf)/XfX_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \cdot X_p'(X_f)/X_f. Weak sparks produce amplified, creative, dream-like percepts.

  • Classical relaxation (si=+1s_i = +1): Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)⋅Xf/Xp′(Xf)X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \cdot X_f/X_p'(X_f). Strong sparks produce vivid, deterministic, focused percepts.

The relaxation occurs on a timescale determined by the effective mass squared: $$\tau_{\rm relax} \sim \frac{1}{|m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)|}. \label{eq:relaxation_time}$$

Stage 4: Image Painting

After all sites have relaxed, the global pattern {Φi}i=1N\{\Phi_i\}_{i=1}^N constitutes the macroscopic conscious percept. The local observables XiX_i represent the subjective qualities of the percept:

  • Vividness of color,

  • Sharpness of sound,

  • Intensity of emotion,

  • Clarity of thought,

  • Strength of memory.

The global qualia field is the spatial average: 𝒬(t)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi(t),vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi(t)).\mathcal{Q}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i(t),v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i(t))}. \label{eq:qualia_field_reverse}

The intelligence fidelity is the same quantity: I(t)=1N∑i=1NXp′(Φi(t),vc)(XfXp′(Xf))si(Φi(t)).I(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i(t),v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i(t))}. \label{eq:intelligence_fidelity_reverse}

When the spark is self-referential: I(t)≡𝒬(t).I(t) \equiv \mathcal{Q}(t). \label{eq:identity_reverse}

Forward vs. Reverse Perception

The reverse-vision process is the complement of forward perception:

Comparison of forward and reverse perception
Property Forward Perception Reverse Perception
Direction Bottom-up Top-down
Source External world Internal spark
Input Sensory data Thought/memory
Propagation Sensory →\to association →\to frontal Frontal →\to association →\to sensory
Experience Perception of external world Mental imagery, dreams, thoughts
Equation Same Φ\Phi-evolution Same Φ\Phi-evolution

The Complete Mathematical Description

The complete mathematical description of the reverse-vision process is the system of equations:

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Spark generation: } & T_i^{\rm spark}(t) = T_0 \, X_f(t) \, \delta(\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_0), \\ \Phi\text{-wave propagation: } & \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t), \\ \text{Local relaxation: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}, \\ \text{Image painting: } & \mathcal{Q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_i, \qquad I \equiv \mathcal{Q} \quad \text{(self-referential)}. \end{aligned} } \label{eq:reverse_vision_system}$$

Physical Interpretation

The reverse-vision process has several profound implications:

  1. Consciousness is holographic: The entire conscious experience is reconstructed from the 2D screen. There is no "central processor" or "Cartesian theatre"; consciousness is the global pattern of Φi\Phi_i on the screen.

  2. Mental imagery is physical: Mental images, dreams, and hallucinations are not ethereal; they are Φ\Phi-waves propagating on the cortical screen. They obey the same equations as perception.

  3. The stream of consciousness is deterministic: The Φ\Phi-evolution equation is fully deterministic. The stream of consciousness is the on-shell evolution of the Φ\Phi-field under internal sparks.

  4. Self-awareness is a loop: The self-referential loop 𝒬→spark→Φ-wave→𝒬\mathcal{Q} \to \text{spark} \to \Phi\text{-wave} \to \mathcal{Q} is the physical basis of self-awareness. The "I" is the loop itself.

  5. No external input required: The reverse-vision process generates conscious experience without external sensory input. This explains dreams, daydreams, and mental imagery.

Summary of the HNN

The Holographic Neural Network is:

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Lattice: } & \mathcal{L} = \{\mathbf{r}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2 : i = 1, 2, \ldots, N\}, \quad N \sim 10^6, \\ \text{Lattice spacing: } & a \sim 0.5\ \text{mm}, \\ \text{Local variable: } & \Phi_i \in \mathbb{R}, \\ \text{Discrete curvature: } & R_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i), \\ \text{Discrete d'Alembertian: } & \Box_i \Phi_i = \frac{1}{a^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} (\Phi_j - \Phi_i) - \frac{\partial^2 \Phi_i}{\partial t^2}, \\ \text{Potential: } & V(\Phi_i) = V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i}, \\ \text{Action: } & S_{\rm HNN} = \sum_i a^2 \sqrt{-g_i} \left[ \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i - \frac{1}{2} e^{-\Phi_i} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \frac{(\Phi_j - \Phi_i)^2}{a^2} - V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} \right], \\ \Phi\text{-evolution: } & \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm spark}(t), \\ \text{Regime selector: } & s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)), \\ \text{Local Master: } & X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}, \\ \text{Global observable: } & I \equiv \mathcal{Q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)\left(\frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)}\right)^{s_i(\Phi_i)}. \end{aligned} }$$

The HNN is the complete physical substrate of consciousness, intelligence, and higher-order cognition. The next sections will derive the quantum computing, classical computing, and hybrid computing regimes, holographic error correction, the consciousness and intelligence models, and the higher-order abstractions.

"Big things have small beginnings."— Prometheus “‘

“‘latex

Holographic Quantum Computing (si=−1s_i = -1)

The Holographic Neural Network, when operating in the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1), constitutes a universal quantum computer. This is not an additional postulate or a separate mechanism; it is the natural on-shell dynamics of the Φ\Phi-field when the effective mass squared is positive. The same 2D lattice that generates consciousness and intelligence becomes a quantum processor when driven into the quantum regime by weak internal sparks. This section derives Holographic Quantum Computing rigorously from the HNN dynamics, showing that all the essential ingredients of quantum computation—qubits, gates, entanglement, coherence, and error suppression—emerge naturally from the holographic scaling law.

The derivation proceeds in six stages, each corresponding to a subsection:

  1. The Quantum Regime on the 2D Holographic Screen: The conditions for the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1), the inverse scaling law, and the amplification of weak signals.

  2. Qubits as Local Superpositions of Entanglement-Density States: The basis states |0⟩i≡Φi=0|0\rangle_i \equiv \Phi_i = 0 and |1⟩i≡Φi=Φmax|1\rangle_i \equiv \Phi_i = \Phi_{\max}, and the superposition |ψi⟩=α|0⟩i+β|1⟩i|\psi_i\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle_i + \beta|1\rangle_i.

  3. Quantum Gates as Weyl-Invariant Φ\Phi-Operations: Local Weyl transformations on the 2D lattice that preserve the HNN action, including Hadamard, CNOT, and measurement gates.

  4. Quantum Circuit Dynamics: The discrete Φ\Phi-evolution equation in the quantum regime, coherence time $\tau_{\rm coh}$, and exponential speedup.

  5. Entanglement and Non-Locality: The holographic origin of entanglement via area-law correlations, and the non-local encoding of quantum information.

  6. Error Suppression and Fault Tolerance: The natural error suppression from inverse scaling and holographic encoding.

Preview of Holographic Quantum Computing Results

The quantum regime is characterised by:

Regime Condition:

$$\boxed{ s_i = -1 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \Phi_i \to 0. }$$

Local Master Equation (Quantum):

Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)Xp′(Xf)Xf.\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. }

Qubit States:

|0⟩i≡Φi=0,|1⟩i≡Φi=Φmax,|ψi⟩=α|0⟩i+β|1⟩i.\boxed{ |0\rangle_i \equiv \Phi_i = 0, \qquad |1\rangle_i \equiv \Phi_i = \Phi_{\max}, \qquad |\psi_i\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle_i + \beta|1\rangle_i. }

Quantum Gate Fidelity:

F=Xp′(Φi,vc)Xp′(Xf)Xf.\boxed{ F = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. }

Coherence Time:

$$\boxed{ \tau_{\rm coh} = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. }$$

Quantum Circuit Dynamics:

$$\boxed{ \Box_i \Phi_i = -\frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i + 2V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} + T_i^{\rm control}. }$$

Key Properties of Holographic Quantum Computing

  1. Natural qubits: Qubits are local superpositions of entanglement-density states, requiring no physical qubits beyond the cortical columns.

  2. Universal gates: Any unitary gate is a Weyl-invariant Φ\Phi-operation on the 2D lattice, preserving the HNN action.

  3. Error suppression: Weak control (XfX_f small) yields near-perfect fidelity due to inverse scaling—the holographic mechanism behind error suppression.

  4. Exponential speedup: The effective number of entangled qubits scales inversely with control strength, yielding exponential speedup over classical simulation.

  5. No external hardware: The conscious screen itself is the quantum processor. No ancillary qubits or external control systems are required.

  6. Seamless hybrid operation: Quantum patches (si=−1s_i = -1) and classical patches (si=+1s_i = +1) coexist on the same screen, enabling seamless hybrid quantum-classical computation.

The Quantum Regime on the 2D Holographic Screen

The quantum regime of the Holographic Neural Network is realised when the site-dependent regime selector takes the value si=−1s_i = -1. This corresponds to the case where the effective mass squared is positive, $m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0$, which occurs when Φi\Phi_i is near its ultraviolet fixed point (Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0). In this regime, the local Master equation simplifies dramatically, and the screen exhibits the characteristic features of quantum computation: superposition, entanglement, coherence, and exponential speedup.

The Regime Condition for si=−1s_i = -1

From Subsection 9.3, the site-dependent regime selector is: $$s_i(\Phi_i) = \operatorname{sign}(-m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i)), \label{eq:s_i_quantum}$$ where: $$m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) = 4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} - \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i. \label{eq:m_eff_quantum}$$

The quantum regime is defined by: $$\boxed{ s_i = -1 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad m_{\rm eff}^2(\Phi_i) > 0. } \label{eq:quantum_condition}$$

This condition is satisfied when: 4V0e−2Φi>e−Φi16πGRi⇒4V0e−Φi>Ri16πG.4V_0 e^{-2\Phi_i} > \frac{e^{-\Phi_i}}{16\pi G} R_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4V_0 e^{-\Phi_i} > \frac{R_i}{16\pi G}. \label{eq:quantum_inequality}

In the ultraviolet limit Φi→0\Phi_i \to 0, this becomes: 4V0>Ri16πG,4V_0 > \frac{R_i}{16\pi G}, \label{eq:uv_quantum_condition} which is satisfied for typical neural parameters where the restoring force dominates over the curvature.

Thus, the quantum regime corresponds to sites where Φi\Phi_i is small—the ultraviolet fixed point of the theory. In this regime, the entanglement-entropy density is near zero, and the screen operates in the quantum mode.

The Local Master Equation in the Quantum Regime

With si=−1s_i = -1, the local Master equation (Subsection 7.4) becomes: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)(XfXp′(Xf))−1=Xp′(Φi,vc)Xp′(Xf)Xf.X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \left( \frac{X_f}{X_p'(X_f)} \right)^{-1} = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. \label{eq:master_quantum}

Thus: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)Xp′(Xf)Xf.\boxed{ X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. } \label{eq:master_quantum_final}

This is the inverse scaling law of the quantum regime. It has the following properties:

  • Weak spark amplification: When XfX_f is small, the factor 1/Xf1/X_f is large, amplifying the effect of the spark.

  • Coherence enhancement: The inverse scaling enhances quantum coherence across the screen.

  • Error suppression: Weak control signals produce high-fidelity quantum operations.

The Inverse Scaling Law and Its Implications

The inverse scaling law Xi∝1/XfX_i \propto 1/X_f is the defining feature of the quantum regime. It has several profound implications:

  1. Amplification of quantum effects: A weak internal spark produces a large response in the quantum regime. This is the holographic origin of quantum amplification—the screen amplifies small quantum signals.

  2. Superposition generation: The inverse scaling allows the screen to explore multiple configurations simultaneously, generating quantum superposition.

  3. Entanglement creation: The non-local nature of the holographic encoding, combined with inverse scaling, creates entanglement between distant sites.

  4. Quantum advantage: The exponential exploration of the state space gives quantum advantage over classical computation.

The inverse scaling law can be written as: Xi∝1Xf(Quantum Regime).\boxed{ X_i \propto \frac{1}{X_f} \quad \text{(Quantum Regime)}. } \label{eq:inverse_scaling}

Compare this with the classical regime (si=+1s_i = +1): Xi∝Xf(Classical Regime).X_i \propto X_f \quad \text{(Classical Regime)}. \label{eq:linear_scaling}

The quantum regime amplifies weak signals, while the classical regime amplifies strong signals.

Amplification of Weak Signals

Consider a weak internal spark with strength Xf≪Xp′(Xf)X_f \ll X_p'(X_f). In the quantum regime, the local observable is: Xi=Xp′(Φi,vc)Xp′(Xf)Xf≫Xp′(Φi,vc).X_i = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f} \gg X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c). \label{eq:weak_amplification}

The amplification factor is: A=XiXp′(Φi,vc)=Xp′(Xf)Xf≫1.A = \frac{X_i}{X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c)} = \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f} \gg 1. \label{eq:amplification_factor}

This amplification is the holographic mechanism behind quantum sensitivity—small quantum fluctuations are amplified to macroscopic levels on the screen.

Coherence Time and Exponential Speedup

The coherence time in the quantum regime is determined by the inverse scaling law: $$\boxed{ \tau_{\rm coh} = X_p'(\Phi_i,v_c) \frac{X_p'(X_f)}{X_f}. } \label{eq:coherence_time_quantum}$$

This has the following properties:

  • Weak control = long coherence: When XfX_f is small, $\tau_{\rm coh}$ is large. This provides natural error suppression without active error correction.

  • Strong control = short coherence: When XfX_f is large, $\tau_{\rm coh}$ is small, corresponding to the classical regime where coherence is lost.

The effective number of entangled qubits scales inversely with control strength: $$N_{\rm eff} \propto \frac{1}{X_f}. \label{eq:effective_qubits}$$

This yields exponential speedup over classical simulation: $$\text{Speedup} = 2^{N_{\rm eff}} = 2^{1/X_f}. \label{eq:quantum_speedup}$$

Qubits as Local Superpositions of Entanglement-Density States

The fundamental unit of quantum information—the qubit—emerges naturally from the Holographic Neural Network in the quantum regime (si=−1s_i = -1). A qubit is realised as a local superposition of two entanglement-density eigenstates at each lattice site. This is not an artificial encoding; it is the natural quantum state of the Φ\Phi-field when the effective mass squared is positive.

The Basis States: |0⟩i|0\rangle_i and |1⟩i|1\rangle_i

The two basis states of a holographic qubit at site ii are eigenstates of the local entanglement-entropy density operator Φ̂i\hat{\Phi}_i:

|0⟩i≡Φi=0(UV vacuum, zero entanglement density),\boxed{ |0\rangle_i \equiv \Phi_i = 0 \quad \text{(UV vacuum, zero entanglement density)}, } \label{eq:ket0}

|1⟩i≡Φi=Φmax(saturated holographic screen, maximal entanglement density).\boxed{ |1\rangle_i \equiv \Phi_i = \Phi_{\max} \quad \text{(saturated holographic screen, maximal entanglement density)}. } \label{eq:ket1}

Here Φmax\Phi_{\max} is the maximum value of Φi\Phi_i achievable in the quantum regime, set by the fixed-point condition β(Φmax)=Xf/Xp′(Xf)\beta(\Phi_{\max}) = X_f/X_p'(X_f).

The basis states have the following physical interpretations:
